topics.title: Congenital Dyserythropoietic Anaemia
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 25 Alignment: Anaemia and Red Cell Disorders → Congenital Dyserythropoietic Anaemia
FCPS/MRCP Focus: CDA Types I/II/III, Morphology (Binuclear erythroblasts), Genetics (SEC23B, KIF23, KLF1), Iron overload, Splenectomy

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define Congenital Dyserythropoietic Anaemia (CDA): Inherited anaemias with ineffective erythropoiesis, morphological abnormalities in erythroblasts
  • [ ] Classify CDA Types I, II, III: Morphology, Genetics, Clinical features
  • [ ] Recognise Key Morphology: Binuclear erythroblasts (CDA II), Multinuclear erythroblasts (CDA I/III)
  • [ ] Apply Genetics: CDA I (CDAN1/C15orf41), CDA II (SEC23B), CDA III (KIF23), CDA IV (KLF1)
  • [ ] Manage: Folic acid, Transfusions if severe, Iron chelation (if iron overload), Splenectomy (CDA II), Allogeneic HSCT (severe)
  • [ ] Differentiate from: Thalassaemia, Sickle Cell, Hereditary Spherocytosis, MDS

📖 Definition & Classification

TypeSynonymKey MorphologyGeneInheritance
CDA IMegaloblastic CDAMultinuclear erythroblasts (≥3 nuclei), Internuclear chromatin bridgesCDAN1 (C15orf41)Autosomal Recessive
CDA IIHEMPAS (Hereditary Erythroblast Multinuclearity with Positive Acidified Serum test)Binuclear erythroblasts (30-50%), Glycosylation defectSEC23BAutosomal Recessive
CDA IIIErythroblast MultinuclearityMultinuclear erythroblasts (up to 12 nuclei), Giant erythroblastsKIF23Autosomal Dominant
CDA IVKLF1-relatedNormal/Minimal dyserythropoiesis, No binuclear cellsKLF1Autosomal Dominant
[!tip] CDA = Ineffective erythropoiesis + Morphological abnormalities. CDA II = Binuclear erythroblasts (HEMPAS). CDA I/III = Multinuclear erythroblasts. SEC23B = CDA II, CDAN1 = CDA I.

⚙️ Pathophysiology

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Genetic Mutation
↓
Defective Erythroblast Division/Maturation
↓
& C2 & C3 & C4
CDA II: SEC23B → Trafficking/Glycosylation Defect → Binuclear Erythroblasts, ↓ Band 3
CDA III: KIF23 → Cytokinesis Defect → Multinuclear Erythroblasts
CDA IV: KLF1 → Transcriptional Dysregulation
↓
Ineffective Erythropoiesis
↓
Increased Erythroblast Apoptosis
↓
Anaemia + Haemolysis + Iron Overload

🔬 Diagnostic Workup

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Congenital Anaemia + Family History + Dyserythropoiesis
↓
**CBC + Film**
↓
**Dyserythropoietic Features?**
↓
**Bone Marrow Aspirate**
↓
**Key Morphology**
↓
**CDA II Suspected**
**CDA I / III Suspected**
↓
**SEC23B Sequencing**
↓
**Genetic Confirmation**
↓
**Serum Test: Acidified Serum Lysis (HEMPAS)**
↓
**CDA II Confirmed**
**CDA I/III/IV**

Key Investigations

TestCDA ICDA II (HEMPAS)CDA IIICDA IV
Binuclear ErythroblastsRare30-50% (Hallmark)RareNone
Multinuclear Erythroblasts (≥3 nuclei)CommonRareCommon (Up to 12 nuclei)None
Acidified Serum Test (HEMPAS)NegativePositiveNegativeNegative
Electron MicroscopyNuclear membrane foldsMembrane abnormalitiesNormalNormal
GeneticsCDAN1 (C15orf41)SEC23BKIF23KLF1
InheritanceARARADAD

🩺 Clinical Features

FeatureCDA ICDA IICDA IIICDA IV
Age at PresentationChildhood/AdolescenceChildhood/Young AdultAdulthoodVariable
Anaemia SeverityModerate-SevereMild-ModerateMildMild
JaundiceCommonCommonMildMild
SplenomegalyCommonCommonMildMild
GallstonesCommonCommonUncommonUncommon
Iron OverloadSevere (Early)Moderate-SevereMildAbsent
Skeletal AbnormalitiesCommon (Scapulae, Digits)RareRareNone
[!warning] Iron Overload is a major complication in CDA I and II (due to ineffective erythropoiesis + transfusions) → Requires Chelation.

🔬 Key Diagnostic Tests

CDA II (HEMPAS) - Most Common

TestFinding
Binuclear Erythroblasts30-50% on BM film
Acidified Serum Lysis Test (HEMPAS)Positive (Lysis of own RBCs in acidified serum)
SDS-PAGEBand 3 deficiency (Reduced), Increased Polypeptides
SEC23B SequencingConfirmatory

Acidified Serum Test (Ham's Test for CDA II - HEMPAS)

PrincipleRBCs from CDA II patients Lyze in Acidified Serum (pH 6.5-7.0) due to Abnormal Band 3
MechanismDefective Band 3 Glycosylation → Exposure of Cryptic Antigen → Complement activation in low pH
SpecificityPositive in CDA II only (Negative in CDA I, III, IV, PNH)

💊 Management

InterventionCDA ICDA IICDA IIICDA IV
Folic Acid5 mg daily (All types)
TransfusionsIf Hb <70-80 g/L / SymptomaticIntermittentRarely neededRarely needed
Iron ChelationEarly/Severe (Ferritin >1000, LIC >7)Moderate-Severe (Ferritin >1000)MildNot needed
SplenectomyControversial (Limited benefit)Beneficial (↑ Hb, ↓ transfusion)Not indicatedNot indicated
Allogeneic HSCTSevere, Transfusion-dependentSevere, Transfusion-dependentRarely neededNot indicated
Interferon-αExperimentalSometimes usedNoNo

🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDifferentiating Features
ThalassaemiaMicrocytic, Target cells, Normal BM maturation (no dyserythropoiesis)
Sickle Cell DiseaseSickle cells, HbS on HPLC, No dyserythropoiesis
Hereditary SpherocytosisSpherocytes, EMA↓/OFT↑, No binuclear/multinuclear erythroblasts
MDSDysplasia ≥10% in ≥1 lineage, Cytogenetic abnormalities, Older age
PNHFlow CD55/CD59-, Haemoglobinuria, No dyserythropoiesis

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
CDA DefinitionInherited + Ineffective erythropoiesis + Dyserythropoiesis
CDA II (HEMPAS)Most common; Binuclear erythroblasts (30-50%), Acidified serum test +ve, SEC23B, Band 3↓
CDA IMultinuclear erythroblasts (≥3 nuclei), Negative HEMPAS, CDAN1, Skeletal abnormalities
CDA IIIMultinuclear (up to 12 nuclei), KIF23, AD inheritance, Adult onset
CDA IVKLF1 mutation, No binuclear/multinuclear cells, AD
Iron OverloadMajor complication (CDA I/II) → Chelation (Deferasirox/Deferiprone/Deferoxamine)
SplenectomyBeneficial in CDA II (↑ Hb, ↓ transfusions)
Acidified Serum Test (HEMPAS)Positive = CDA II (Specific)

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the hallmark morphological feature of CDA II (HEMPAS)?
  • Binuclear erythroblasts (30-50%) on bone marrow aspirate
  1. What is the Acidified Serum Lysis Test (HEMPAS) and when is it positive?
  • Patient's RBCs lyse in acidified serum (pH 6.5-7.0); Positive only in CDA II (Specific)
  1. How does CDA II differ from CDA I morphologically?
  • CDA II: Binuclear erythroblasts (30-50%); CDA I: Multinuclear erythroblasts (≥3 nuclei)
  1. What is the molecular basis of CDA II (HEMPAS)?
  • SEC23B mutation → Defective glycosylation/trafficking → Band 3 deficiency
  1. Why does iron overload occur in CDA and how is it managed?
  • Ineffective erythropoiesis + Transfusions → Iron overload → Chelation (Deferasirox/Deferiprone/Deferoxamine)
  1. Is Splenectomy beneficial in CDA?
  • CDA II: Yes (↑ Hb, ↓ transfusion need); CDA I: Controversial; CDA III/IV: Not indicated
  1. What is the Acidified Serum Test (HEMPAS) and what does it detect?
  • Patient's RBCs incubated in acidified serum (pH 6.5-7.0) → Lysis = Positive for CDA II (Band 3 defect)
  1. What genetic mutations cause CDA I, II, III, and IV?
  • CDA I: CDAN1/C15orf41; CDA II: SEC23B; CDA III: KIF23; CDA IV: KLF1
  1. How does CDA differ from Thalassaemia?
  • CDA: Dyserythropoiesis (Binuclear/Multinuclear erythroblasts), Normal/High MCV; Thalassaemia: Microcytic, Target cells, No dyserythropoiesis
  1. What is the inheritance pattern of each CDA type?
  • CDA I: AR, CDA II: AR, CDA III: AD, CDA IV: AD
  1. What is the role of Interferon-α in CDA?
  • Sometimes used in CDA I (Experimental), CDA II (Sometimes), Not in III/IV
  1. How do you diagnose CDA IV?
  • KLF1 mutation, No binuclear/multinuclear erythroblasts, AD inheritance, Mild anaemia

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
CDA I vs CDA IICDA I = Multinuclear (≥3 nuclei); CDA II = Binuclear (30-50%)
HEMPAS TestPositive = CDA II only; Negative = CDA I, III, IV, PNH
SEC23B vs CDAN1SEC23B = CDA II; CDAN1 = CDA I
Iron OverloadCDA I/II = Severe; CDA III = Mild; CDA IV = None
SplenectomyBeneficial in CDA II; Controversial in CDA I
MnemonicMeaning
"CDA II = Binuclear = 2 Nuclei = SEC23B"CDA II hallmarks
"CDA I = Multi-Nuclear = 3+ Nuclei = CDAN1"CDA I hallmarks
"CDA III = Many Nuclei (12) = KIF23"CDA III hallmarks
"CDA IV = KLF1 = No Dyserythropoiesis"CDA IV unique
"HEMPAS = CDA II Only = Acidified Serum Lysis"Specific test
"SEC23B = CDA II = Band 3 Defect"Molecular mechanism

topics.title: Cutaneous T Cell Lymphoma
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 25 Alignment: Haematological Malignancies → Lymphomas → Cutaneous T-cell Lymphoma
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Mycosis fungoides (MF), Sézary syndrome (SS), TNMB staging, skin-directed vs systemic therapy, prognosis, HTLV-1 association

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define Cutaneous T-cell Lymphoma (CTCL): Primary cutaneous T-cell lymphoma with epidermotropism
  • [ ] Distinguish Mycosis Fungoides (MF) vs Sézary Syndrome (SS): MF = Skin-limited patches/plaques; SS = Erythroderma + Leukaemic phase (>1000 Sézary cells/µL)
  • [ ] Apply TNMB Staging (IASLC/ISCL/EORTC): T (skin), N (nodes), M (visceral), B (blood)
  • [ ] Manage Early MF (IA-IIA): Skin-directed (Topical steroids, PUVA, Radiotherapy, Mechlorethamine)
  • [ ] Manage Advanced MF/SS (IIB-IV): Systemic therapy (Retinoids, Interferon-α, HDAC inhibitors, PDT, Mogamulizumab, Brentuximab)
  • [ ] Recognise Sézary Syndrome: Erythroderma + Lymphadenopathy + >1000 Sézary cells/µL (CD4+CD7-CD26-)
  • [ ] Understand Prognosis: Early MF = Normal life expectancy; SS = Median OS 2-4 years

📖 Definition & Classification

EntityDefinitionKey Features
Mycosis Fungoides (MF)Most common CTCL (~60%); Epidermotropic CD4+ T-cells; Patches → Plaques → TumoursSlow progression, Skin-limited early
Sézary Syndrome (SS)Leukaemic variant; Erythroderma + Sézary cells >1000/µL (CD4+CD7-CD26-)Aggressive, Blood involvement
Primary Cutaneous CD30+ LPDLyP (Lymphomatoid Papulosis) + pcALCLCD30+, Good prognosis
[!tip] MF = Patches/Plaques/Tumours (Skin-limited early); SS = Erythroderma + Blood involvement (>1000 Sézary/µL). Sézary cells = CD4+CD7-CD26-.

⚙️ Pathophysiology

⬇ Clinical Pathway
CD4+ T-cell Clone
↓
Cutaneous Homing (CCR4, CLA)
↓
Epidermotropism (MF) / Blood Circulation (SS)
↓
& D2
SS: Erythroderma + Blood Involvement
↓
Loss of CD7 / CD26 / CD62L
↓
Immune Evasion / ↓ Tumour Surveillance

🔬 Diagnostic Workup

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Chronic Rash/Erythroderma + Lymphadenopathy
↓
**Skin Biopsy (Punch/Incisional)**
↓
**Histology: Epidermotropism, Pautrier Microabscesses, Cerebriform Nuclei**
↓
**IHC: CD3+, CD4+, CD45RO+, CD7-, CD26- (MF/SS)**
↓
**Blood: Sézary Count (Flow: CD4+CD7-CD26-)**
↓
**Sézary >1000/µL?**
↓
& H
**Mycosis Fungoides**
↓
**Staging: TNMB**
↓
**CT Chest/Abd/Pelvis**
↓
**CT/PET-CT**
↓
**BM Biopsy** (if Stage ≥IIB)
↓
**TCR Gene Rearrangement (PCR)**

Essential Investigations

TestMFSS
Skin BiopsyEpidermotropism, Pautrier microabscesses, Cerebriform nucleiSimilar (often less epidermotropism)
IHCCD3+, CD4+, CD45RO+, CD7-, CD26-, CD30- (usually)CD3+, CD4+, CD7-, CD26-, CD30- (variable)
Sézary Count (Flow)<1000/µL>1000/µL (CD4+CD7-CD26-)
CXCR3/CCR4CCR4+ (Skin homing)CCR4+
TCR RearrangementClonal (PCR)Clonal (Same clone as skin)
Lymph Node BiopsyIf ≥1.5cm (Dermatopathic vs Involved)Often involved
BM BiopsyStage IIB+Usually involved
HTLV-1 SerologyNegative (usually)Positive in ATLL (Differential)

📊 TNMB Staging (IASLC/ISCL/EORTC)

CategoryStageCriteria
T (Skin)T1Patches/Plagues <10% BSA
T2Patches/Plagues ≥10% BSA
T3Tumours (≥1cm)
T4Erythroderma
N (Nodes)N0No lymphadenopathy
N1Dermatopathic changes
N2Lymphomatous involvement
N3Lymphomatous + Masses
M (Visceral)M0No visceral involvement
M1Visceral involvement
B (Blood)B0<5% Sézary cells
B15-100/µL (Low)
B2>1000/µL (Sézary Syndrome)

Stage Grouping

StageTNMBMedian Survival
IAT1, N0, M0, B0Normal
IBT2, N0, M0, B0Normal
IIAT1-2, N1, M0, B0~15-20 years
IIBT3, N0-1, M0, B0~5-10 years
IIIT4, N0-1, M0, B0-1Erythroderma
IVA1T1-4, N0-2, M0, B2Sézary Syndrome (~3-5 years)
IVA2T1-4, N3, M0, B0-2~2-4 years
IVBAny T, Any N, M1, Any B<2 years

💊 Management

Early Stage (IA-IIA) - Skin-Directed Therapy

ModalityIndicationDetails
Topical CorticosteroidsT1-T2 (Patches/Plagues)Potent/Ultrapotent (Clobetasol), Taper to maintenance
Phototherapy (PUVA/Narrowband UVB)T1-T2 (Widespread)2-3x/week, Clears ~70-80%, Maintenance
Topical Mechlorethamine (Nitrogen Mustard)T1-T20.01-0.02% gel, Daily → Taper, Carcinogenic risk
Radiotherapy (Localised/Total Skin Electron Beam)T1-T3 (Localised/TST)TSEB: 12-36 Gy, Excellent local control
Topical Bexarotene (Retinoid)T1-T21% gel, Daily, Skin irritation common

Advanced Stage (IIB-IV) - Systemic Therapy

AgentIndicationKey Points
Oral Bexarotene (Retinoid)IIB-IV300 mg/m²/day, Lipids/Thyroid monitoring, Triglycerides ↑, Hypothyroidism
Interferon-αIIB-IV3-5 MU SC 3x/week, Flu-like, Depression, Cytopenias
HDAC Inhibitors (Vorinostat, Romidepsin)Relapsed/RefractoryOral/IV, Fatigue, Nausea, Thrombocytopenia, QTc prolongation (Romidepsin)
Mogamulizumab (Anti-CCR4)Relapsed/Refractory (SS/MF)Anti-CCR4 mAb, 1mg/kg IV q1-2wk, Rash, Infusion reactions, Autoimmune
Brentuximab VedotinCD30+ MF/SSAnti-CD30 ADC, 1.8mg/kg IV q3wk, Peripheral neuropathy
Extracorporeal Photopheresis (ECP)SS / Erythrodermic MFLeukapheresis + 8-MOP + UVA, 2 days q2-4wk, First-line for SS
Allogeneic HSCTYoung, Fit, RefractoryOnly potentially curative

🩺 Sézary Syndrome Specifics

FeatureDetails
DiagnosisErythroderma + Lymphadenopathy + Sézary cells >1000/µL (CD4+CD7-CD26-)
Sézary CellsCerebriform nuclei, CD4+CD7-CD26-CD62L-
Erythroderma>80% BSA, Pruritus, Ectropion, Palmoplantar keratoderma, Lymphadenopathy
First-lineECP (Extracorporeal Photopheresis) + Retinoids/Interferon
PrognosisMedian OS 2-4 years (worse than MF)

🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionKey Differentiators
Sézary vs ATLLATLL: HTLV-1+, Flower cells, Hypercalcaemia, Lytic lesions; SS: HTLV-1-, Cerebriform, No hypercalcaemia
Sézary vs Erythrodermic MFSS: Sézary cells >1000/µL + Erythroderma; MF: <1000, May have erythroderma (T4)
MF vs Eczema/PsoriasisBiopsy: Epidermotropism, Pautrier microabscesses, Cerebriform nuclei, Clonal TCR
MF vs ParapsoriasisParapsoriasis: No atypia, No clonality, Benign course
SS vs Erythrodermic CTCLSS = Erythroderma + >1000 Sézary/µL (Specific leukaemic phase)
CD30+ LPD (LyP/pcALCL)CD30+, CD30+ large cells, Self-regressing (LyP)

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
MF vs SSMF = Patches/Plaques/Tumours (<1000 Sézary); SS = Erythroderma + >1000 Sézary/µL
Sézary CellsCD4+CD7-CD26-CD62L-, Cerebriform nuclei
StagingTNMB: T1-4 (Skin), N0-3 (Nodes), M0-1 (Visceral), B0-2 (Blood)
Early MF (IA-IIA)Skin-directed: Topical steroids, PUVA, Radiotherapy, Mechlorethamine
Advanced (IIB-IV)Systemic: Bexarotene, IFN-α, HDAC inhibitors, Mogamulizumab, Brentuximab, ECP
Sézary SyndromeErythroderma + >1000 Sézary/µL, ECP first-line, Median OS 2-4yr
PrognosisEarly MF = Normal life expectancy; SS = 2-4 years
Key IHCCD4+, CD7-, CD26- (Sézary/MF), CD30- (MF), CD30+ (ALCL/pcALCL)

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the difference between Mycosis Fungoides and Sézary Syndrome?
  • MF: Patches/Plques/Tumours, Sézary cells <1000/µL; SS: Erythroderma + >1000 Sézary/µL, Leukaemic phase
  1. What are Sézary cells and their immunophenotype?
  • Cerebriform nuclei, CD4+CD7-CD26-CD62L-
  1. What is the TNMB staging for CTCL?
  • T1-4 (Skin), N0-3 (Nodes), M0-1 (Visceral), B0-2 (Blood Sézary count)
  1. What is the first-line treatment for early stage Mycosis Fungoides (IA-IIA)?
  • Skin-directed therapy: Topical steroids, PUVA, Radiotherapy, Topical Mechlorethamine
  1. What is the standard first-line treatment for Sézary Syndrome?
  • Extracorporeal Photopheresis (ECP) + Retinoids/Interferon-α
  1. What is the immunophenotype of Sézary cells?
  • CD4+CD7-CD26-CD62L-, Cerebriform nuclei
  1. How does Sézary Syndrome differ from ATLL?
  • ATLL: HTLV-1+, Flower cells, Hypercalcaemia, Lytic lesions; SS: HTLV-1-, Cerebriform, No Hypercalcaemia
  1. What is the prognosis of Stage IA Mycosis Fungoides vs Sézary Syndrome?
  • Stage IA MF: Normal life expectancy; Sézary Syndrome: Median OS 2-4 years
  1. What is the role of Extracorporeal Photopheresis (ECP) in CTCL?
  • First-line for Sézary Syndrome, Erythrodermic MF; Mechanism: Immunomodulation, apoptosis of malignant T-cells
  1. What is the difference between MF and Parapsoriasis?
  • MF: Atypia, Pautrier microabscesses, Cerebriform nuclei, Clonal TCR; Parapsoriasis: No atypia, Benign, No clonality
  1. What is the role of Mogamulizumab in CTCL?
  • Anti-CCR4 antibody, Relapsed/Refractory MF/SS, Rash, Infusion reactions, Autoimmune
  1. How is Erythrodermic MF (T4) staged and treated?
  • Stage III-IVA1; Systemic therapy (Retinoids, IFN-α, ECP, Bexarotene)

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
MF vs SSMF = Patches/Plaques/Tumours, Sézary <1000; SS = Erythroderma, Sézary >1000
MF vs ParapsoriasisMF: Atypia, Pautrier microabscesses, Clonal TCR; Parapsoriasis: No atypia, No clonality
SS vs Erythrodermic MF (T4)SS = >1000 Sézary/µL + Erythroderma; T4 MF = Erythroderma, <1000 Sézary
SS vs ATLLATLL = HTLV-1+, Flower cells, Hypercalcaemia; SS = HTLV-1-, Cerebriform, No HyperCa
PCALCL vs LyP vs MFALCL: CD30+, ALK+/-; LyP: CD30+, Self-regressing; MF: CD30-, Epidermotropic
MnemonicMeaning
"MF = Mycosis Fungoides = Patches→Plaques→Tumours"MF progression
"SS = Sézary = Erythroderma + >1000 Sézary"SS definition
"Sézary = CD4, CD7-, CD26-, CD62L-"Immunophenotype
"ECP = First-line SS"Treatment
"TNMB = T(Skin) N(Node) M(Visceral) B(Blood)"Staging
"Early MF = Skin-directed; Advanced = Systemic"Treatment paradigm

🗺️ Mind Map

Cutaneous T-cell Lymphoma
Mycosis Fungoides (MF)
Patches → Plaques → Tumours
CD4+, CD7-, CD26-
Pautrier Microabscesses
Early: Skin-Directed (Steroids, PUVA, RT, Mechlorethamine)
Advanced: Systemic (Bexarotene, IFN-α, HDACi, Mogamulizumab, Brentuximab, ECP)
Sézary Syndrome (SS)
Erythroderma + >1000 Sézary/µL
CD4+CD7-CD26-CD62L-
Cerebriform Nuclei
ECP First-line
Bexarotene, IFN-α, HDACi
Median OS 2-4yr
Staging (TNMB)
T1-4, N0-3, M0-1, B0-2
Differential
ATLL: HTLV-1+, Flower Cells, HyperCa
Erythrodermic MF: <1000 Sézary
CD30+ LPD: CD30+
Treatment
Early: Skin-Directed
Advanced: Systemic
SS: ECP

📋 One-Page Revision Card

CUTANEOUS T-CELL LYMPHOMA – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
MF: Patches→Plaques→Tumours, Sézary <1000/µL
SS: Erythroderma + >1000 Sézary/µL (CD4+CD7-CD26-CD62L-)
Staging TNMB: T(Skin)1-4, N(Nodes)0-3, M(Visceral)0-1, B(Blood)0-2
Early IA-IIA: Skin-Directed (Topical Steroids, PUVA, RT, Mechlorethamine)
Advanced IIB-IV: Systemic (Bexarotene, IFN-α, HDACi, Mogamulizumab, Brentuximab, ECP)
SS First-line: ECP (Extracorporeal Photopheresis)
Prognosis: MF IA-IIA = Normal life expectancy; SS = 2-4yr median OS
IHC: CD4+, CD7-, CD26- (MF/SS); CD30- (MF), CD30+ (ALCL)
Differential: ATLL (HTLV-1+, Flower cells, HyperCa), Parapsoriasis (No atypia)

🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowMF vs SS definition, Sézary cell immunophenotype (CD4+CD7-CD26-), TNMB staging, Early MF = skin-directed, SS = ECP first-line, MF/SS prognosis difference, MF/SS immunophenotype (CD4+, CD7-, CD26-), ATLL differentiation
Should KnowTNMB staging details, Skin-directed therapy options (PUVA, Mechlorethamine, RT), Systemic agents (Bexarotene, IFN-α, HDACi, Mogamulizumab, Brentuximab), ECP mechanism, Ceritinib in CTCL, ATLL vs SS, Erythrodermic MF vs SS distinction, Prognostic factors (Large cell transformation, LDH, B2M)
Nice to KnowTCR Vβ repertoire analysis, KIR3DL2 as Sézary marker, CCR4 as therapeutic target (Mogamulizumab), FCRL3 as Sézary marker, Gene expression profiling (GERP), CAR-T in CTCL, Photodynamic therapy, Radiotherapy techniques (TSEB, Tomotherapy), Cost-effectiveness, CAR-T in CTCL, Novel agents (Lacutamab, Ipatasertib), Clinical trial landscape

topics.title: Cytogenetics Molecular Diagnostics
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 25 Alignment: Haematology Investigations → Cytogenetics & Molecular Diagnostics
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Karyotyping, FISH, PCR, NGS, WHO classification, Prognostic markers, Targeted therapy, ELN/IPSS-R criteria

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Perform Karyotype Analysis: Banding (G-banding), Reading convention (ISCN), Chromosome nomenclature
  • [ ] Apply FISH: Probe Types (Dual-fusion, Break-apart, Deletion), Interphase/Metaphase, Limitations
  • [ ] Apply PCR/qRT-PCR: Qualitative/Quantitative, Fusion Genes, Mutation Detection, MRD
  • [ ] Apply NGS: Targeted Panels, Whole Exome/Genome, Variant Interpretation (VAF, Pathogenicity)
  • [ ] Apply WHO/ICC Classification: Integration of Morphology + Cytogenetics + Molecular
  • [ ] Apply Prognostic Systems: ELN 2022 (AML), IPSS-R/IPSS-M (MDS), R-ISS (Myeloma), IPI (DLBCL)
  • [ ] Interpret MRD by PCR/NGS: Sensitivity, Standardisation, Clinical Utility

📖 Cytogenetics

Karyotyping

AspectDetails
SampleBone Marrow (Heparin) or Peripheral Blood (if high blast count)
Culture24-48h (Unstimulated) + PHA Stimulation (72h) for Lymphocytes
BandingG-banding (Trypsin/Giemsa) - Standard; Q/R-banding (Alternative)
Resolution400-550 Bands (Standard); High-resolution (850+) for Subtle Abnormalities
Analysis20 Metaphases (Standard); 50 (If Mosaicism Suspected)
ReportingISCN 2020 Nomenclature

Chromosome Nomenclature (ISCN 2020)

NotationMeaningExample
p / qShort arm / Long arm7q-
t(A;B)(p1;q2)Translocation between Chr A & Bt(9;22)(q34;q11)
inv(A)(p1q2)Inversion within Chromosome Ainv(16)(p13.1q22)
del(A)(q1q2)Deletiondel(5)(q13q33) / del(5q)
dup(A)(q1q2)Duplicationdup(1q)
ins(A;B)(p1;q2q3)Insertionins(3;21)(q21;q22q22)
+A / -AGain / Loss of Whole Chromosome+8, -7
i(A)(q1) / dic(A;B)Isochromosome / Dicentrici(17q), dic(9;20)
/ Mosaicism / Cell Line46,XX/47,XX,+8

Key Recurrent Abnormalities

AbnormalityDiseaseSignificance
t(9;22)(q34;q11)CML, ALLBCR::ABL1, TKI target
t(15;17)(q24;q21)APML (AML-M3)PML::RARA, ATRA/ATO
t(8;21)(q22;q22)AMLRUNX1::RUNX1T1, Favourable
inv(16)(p13.1q22)AMLCBFB::MYH11, Favourable
t(11;14)(q13;q32)MCLCCND1::IGH, Cyclin D1
t(14;18)(q32;q21)FLIGH::BCL2, BCL2 overexpression
t(11;18)MALTAPI2::MALT1
-5 / del(5q)MDS, AMLPoor Prognosis
-7 / del(7q)MDS, AMLPoor Prognosis
Complex Karyotype (≥3)MDS, AMLAdverse (ELN 2022)
Monosomal KaryotypeMDS, AMLAdverse

🔬 FISH (Fluorescence In Situ Hybridisation)

Probe Types

Probe TypeDesignApplication
Dual-FusionTwo Fusion Signals (Red+Green)BCR::ABL1 (t(9;22)), PML::RARA (t(15;17)), RUNX1::RUNX1T1 (t(8;21))
Break-ApartSplit Signals (Red+Green Separate)KMT2A (11q23), ALK, ETV6, MYC
DeletionLoss of Signal17p/TP53, 13q14 (RB1), 20q, 5q, 7q
Enumeration / Copy NumberCount Signals+8, HER2 Amplification, MYC Amplification

FISH vs Karyotype

FeatureFISHKaryotype
Resolution100 kb - 1 Mb5-10 Mb
Throughput200-500 cells20-50 Metaphases
TargetedYes (Specific Probe)Genome-wide
DetectionInterphase + MetaphaseMetaphase Only
Sensitivity1-5%5-10%
Turnaround24-48h7-14 Days
[!tip] FISH = Rapid, Targeted, Higher Sensitivity; Karyotype = Genome-wide, Lower Resolution, Detects Novel/Complex Changes.

🧬 Molecular Diagnostics

PCR-based Methods

MethodApplicationSensitivity
Qualitative RT-PCRFusion Gene Detection (BCR::ABL1, PML::RARA, CBFB::MYH11)10⁻⁴ - 10⁻⁵
Quantitative RT-PCR (qRT-PCR)MRD (BCR::ABL1 IS, NPM1, CBFB::MYH11)10⁻⁴ - 10⁻⁶
Digital PCR (dPCR)Absolute Quantification (No Standard Curve)10⁻⁵ - 10⁻⁶
Allele-Specific PCRHotspot Mutations (FLT3-ITD, NPM1, JAK2 V617F)10⁻³ - 10⁻⁴
ASO-PCR (Allele-Specific Oligonucleotide)Patient-specific MRD (IGH/TCR Rearrangements)10⁻⁴ - 10⁻⁶

Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)

ApproachApplicationAdvantages
Targeted Panel (50-100 genes)Diagnosis, Prognosis, Therapy Selection (AML, MDS, MPN, Lymphoma)Cost-effective, Deep Coverage (500-1000x), Fast (3-5 days)
Whole Exome (WES)Research, Novel DriversAll Coding Regions, Novel Variants
Whole Genome (WGS)Research, Structural VariantsComplete Genome, SV Detection, Non-coding

NGS Variant Interpretation (ACMG/AMP 2015)

CategoryCriteria
PathogenicPVS1 + PS1-4 + PM1-6 + PP1-5 (≥1 Very Strong + ≥1 Strong OR ≥2 Strong)
Likely PathogenicStrong + Moderate OR ≥3 Moderate
VUS (Uncertain Significance)Insufficient Evidence
Likely BenignBenign Supporting ≥1
BenignBA1 (Population Freq >5%) OR BS1-4

Key Mutations by Disease

DiseaseKey MutationsClinical Utility
AMLFLT3-ITD/TKD, NPM1, CEBPA, IDH1/2, DNMT3A, TP53, ASXL1, RUNX1, SRSF2, U2AF1, KITPrognosis (ELN 2022), Targeted Therapy (Midostaurin, Gilteritinib, Ivosidenib, Enasidenib, Venetoclax)
MDSSF3B1, SRSF2, U2AF1, ZRSR2, ASXL1, RUNX1, TP53, DNMT3A, TET2, EZH2Prognosis (IPSS-M), Luspatercept (SF3B1)
MPNJAK2 V617F, CALR Type 1/2, MPL, TET2, ASXL1, SRSF2, IDH1/2Diagnosis, Prognosis (MIPSS70), Ruxolitinib (JAK2)
ALLETV6::RUNX1, BCR::ABL1, KMT2A-r, TCF3::PBX1, Ph-like (CRLF2, JAK2, EPOR)Risk Stratification, TKI (Ph+), Targeted (Dasatinib, Ruxolitinib)
CLLTP53, ATM, NOTCH1, SF3B1, BIRC3, MYD88, IGHV StatusPrognosis, Treatment Choice (BTKi vs Venetoclax)
DLBCLMYC/BCL2/BCL6 (Double/Triple Hit), EZH2, TP53, BCL2, CARD11, MYD88DH/TH Identification, EZH2mut → Tazemetostat

📊 Integration: WHO/ICC Classification + Prognostic Systems

AML (ELN 2022 Risk Stratification)

RiskGenetics
Favourablet(8;21), inv(16), NPM1mut (no FLT3-ITD or low AR), CEBPAbiallelic
IntermediateNPM1mut + FLT3-ITDhigh, Normal Karyotype, Other
AdverseTP53mut, Complex Karyotype, Monosomal Karyotype, -5/del5q, -7/del7q, inv(3)/t(3;3), KMT2A-r (non-t(9;11)), FLT3-ITDhigh + Adverse Cytogenetics

MDS (IPSS-R / IPSS-M)

IPSS-R VariableScoring
CytogeneticsVery Good (0) → Very Poor (4)
Blasts %≤2% (0), 2-<5% (0.5), 5-<10% (1), 10-<20% (2), >20% (3)
Hb≥10 (0), 8-<10 (1), <8 (1.5)
Plt≥100 (0), 50-<100 (0.5), <50 (1)
ANC≥0.8 (0), <0.8 (0.5)

Multiple Myeloma (R-ISS)

StageCriteria
IISS I + No High-risk FISH (17p-, t(4;14), t(14;16)) + Normal LDH
IINot I or III
IIIISS III (β2M >5.5) + High-risk FISH OR High LDH

🎯 Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) by Molecular Methods

MethodTargetSensitivityStandard
qRT-PCR (Fusion Genes)BCR::ABL1 (IS), PML::RARA, CBFB::MYH11, NPM110⁻⁴ - 10⁻⁶International Scale (IS)
Digital PCRFusion Genes, Mutations10⁻⁵ - 10⁻⁶Absolute Quantification
NGS (Clonotypic)IGH/TCR Rearrangement, Mutations10⁻⁴ - 10⁻⁶Clonotype Tracking
Multiparameter FlowLAIP10⁻⁴ - 10⁻⁵≥1M Events

MRD Clinical Utility

DiseaseMRD Application
CMLBCR::ABL1 IS (qRT-PCR) → TFR Criteria (MR4.5 sustained ≥2y)
AMLNPM1, CBFB::MYH11, FLT3-ITD → Transplant Decision
ALLIGH/TCR Rearrangement, ETV6::RUNX1, BCR::ABL1 → Intensity Adjustment
CLLIGH Clonotype (NGS/dPCR) → Stop Therapy (uMRD)
MyelomaClonotypic NGS (10⁻⁵-10⁻⁶) → Sustained MRD-neg → Treatment Stop

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
KaryotypeISCN, 20 Metaphases, t(9;22), t(15;17), t(8;21), inv(16), -5/-7, Complex/Monosomal = Adverse
FISHDual-Fusion (BCR::ABL1, PML::RARA), Break-Apart (KMT2A, ALK), Deletion (17p, 5q, 7q), Fast, Targeted, Sensitive
PCRqRT-PCR for MRD (BCR::ABL1 IS, NPM1, PML::RARA), Digital PCR for Absolute Quant
NGSTargeted Panels (50-100 genes), VAF, Pathogenicity (ACMG), ELN 2022 / IPSS-M / R-ISS Integration
ELN 2022 AMLFavourable (t8;21, inv16, NPM1/FLT3low, CEBPAbi), Adverse (TP53, Complex, Mono, -5/-7)
IPSS-MIPSS-R + 17 Mutations = Molecular IPSS
R-ISS MyelomaISS + FISH (17p, 4;14, 14;16) + LDH
MRDqRT-PCR (10⁻⁴-10⁻⁶), NGS (10⁻⁴-10⁻⁶), Flow (10⁻⁴-10⁻⁵)
CMLBCR::ABL1 IS → MR4.5 (0.0032%) → TFR
t(9;22)CML/ALL → BCR::ABL1 → TKI Target

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the difference between Karyotype and FISH?
  • Karyotype = Genome-wide, 5-10 Mb resolution, Metaphase only; FISH = Targeted, 100kb-1Mb, Interphase+Metaphase, Higher sensitivity
  1. What are the common FISH probe types and their uses?
  • Dual-Fusion: BCR::ABL1, PML::RARA; Break-Apart: KMT2A, ALK; Deletion: 17p, 5q, 7q; Enumeration: +8
  1. What is the ELN 2022 favourable risk group in AML?
  • t(8;21), inv(16), NPM1mut without FLT3-ITD (or low AR), CEBPAbiallelic
  1. What is the significance of TP53 mutation in MDS/AML?
  • Adverse Prognosis, Resistant to Chemotherapy, IPSS-M Very High Risk, Venetoclax/Azacitidine Preferred
  1. What is the International Scale (IS) for BCR::ABL1 monitoring?
  • Standardised qRT-PCR; Major Molecular Response (MMR) = BCR::ABL1 ≤0.1% IS; MR4.5 = ≤0.0032% (TFR threshold)
  1. How does IPSS-M differ from IPSS-R?
  • IPSS-M adds 17 Molecular Mutations (TP53, FLT3, RAS, Splicing Factors, etc.) → Better Risk Stratification
  1. What is the significance of TP53 mutation in MDS?
  • Worst Prognosis, IPSS-M Very High Risk, Multi-hit TP53 = Adverse, Responds poorly to HMAs, Better with Azacitidine + Eprenetapopt (Trials)
  1. What is the role of Molecular testing in Multiple Myeloma?
  • R-ISS Staging: ISS + High-risk FISH (17p, t(4;14), t(14;16)) + LDH; MRD by NGS (Clonotypic)
  1. What is the difference between qRT-PCR and dPCR for MRD?
  • qRT-PCR = Relative Quant (Standard Curve); dPCR = Absolute Quant (Partitioning), No Standard Curve, Higher Precision
  1. What are the key mutations in AML with prognostic/therapeutic significance?
  • FLT3-ITD/TKD (Midostaurin/Gilteritinib), NPM1 (Prognosis), IDH1/2 (Ivosidenib/Enasidenib), TP53 (Adverse), CEBPA (Favourable if Biallelic)
  1. How does NGS integrate into WHO/ICC classification?
  • Defining Genetic Abnormalities (SF3B1, TP53, NPM1, CEBPA, KMT2A-r) → Diagnostic Criteria; Prognostic Systems (ELN, IPSS-M, R-ISS) = Molecular + Cytogenetic + Clinical |

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Karyotype vs FISHKaryotype = Genome-wide; FISH = Targeted, Faster, More Sensitive
FISH Probe TypesDual-Fusion = Translocation (BCR::ABL1); Break-Apart = Gene Split (KMT2A); Deletion = Loss (17p)
ELN 2022 vs 20172022: TP53 = Adverse regardless; NPM1/FLT3-ITDlow = Favourable
IPSS-R vs IPSS-MIPSS-M = IPSS-R + 17 Mutations
qRT-PCR vs dPCRqRT-PCR = Relative (Std Curve); dPCR = Absolute (Partitioning)
BCR::ABL1 ISStandardised % (not copies); MMR = ≤0.1%, MR4.5 = ≤0.0032%
MnemonicMeaning
"Karyotype = Genome Map"Full chromosome view
"FISH = Flashlight = Targeted"Specific probe
"ELN 2022: Fav = t(8;21), inv16, NPM1/FLT3low, CEBPAbi"Favourable risk
"ELN 2022: Adv = TP53, Complex, Mono, -5/-7"Adverse risk
"IPSS-M = IPSS-R + 17 Genes"Molecular IPSS
"IS = International Scale = Standardised BCR::ABL1"CML monitoring
"MR4.5 = 0.0032% = TFR"Treatment-free remission

🗺️ Mind Map

📋 One-Page Revision Card

CYTOGENETICS & MOLECULAR DIAGNOSTICS – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Karyotype: G-Banding, ISCN, 20 Metaphases; t(9;22), t(15;17), t(8;21), inv16, -5/-7, Complex
FISH: Dual-Fusion (BCR::ABL1, PML::RARA), Break-Apart (KMT2A), Deletion (17p, 5q, 7q)

🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

topics.title: Dic
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 25 Alignment: Bleeding and Thrombotic Disorders → DIC
FCPS/MRCP Focus: ISTH overt DIC score, sepsis/trauma/obstetric triggers, replacement therapy (FFP/cryo/platelets), NO therapeutic heparin, treat underlying cause

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define DIC: Acquired syndrome of systemic intravascular coagulation activation → thrombin generation → fibrin formation → consumption of platelets/coagulation factors + fibrinolysis → bleeding ± thrombosis
  • [ ] Apply ISTH Overt DIC Score: Platelets, Fibrinogen, PT, D-dimer/FDP → ≥5 = Overt DIC
  • [ ] Identify triggers: Sepsis (most common), Trauma, Obstetric (abruption, AFE, retained products), Malignancy, Transplant, Vascular anomalies
  • [ ] Differentiate Overt DIC (bleeding) from Non-overt/Sepsis-induced coagulopathy (SIC) (organ dysfunction, score <5)
  • [ ] Manage: TREAT UNDERLYING CAUSE (antibiotics, delivery, surgery); Replacement if bleeding/invasive procedure; NO routine heparin prophylaxis

📖 Definition & Pathophysiology

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Trigger: Sepsis, Trauma, Obstetric, Cancer
↓
Tissue Factor (TF) Exposure
↓
Extrinsic Pathway Activation
↓
Thrombin Generation
↓
& E2 & E3
Platelet Activation & Consumption
Coagulation Factor Consumption
↓
**Consumptive Coagulopathy**
↓
**Bleeding** (Factor/Platelet Deficiency)
**Microvascular Thrombosis** → Organ Dysfunction
↓
Fibrinolysis Activation (tPA Release)
↓
**D-dimer / FDP Elevation**
TypeMechanismClinical
Acute DICSudden massive TF exposure (sepsis, trauma, obstetric)Bleeding dominant, rapid onset
Chronic DICLow-grade continuous TF (solid tumours, vascular anomalies)Thrombosis dominant, compensated

🔬 Diagnostic Workup – ISTH Overt DIC Score

ParameterScore
Platelet Count>100 = 0; 50-100 = 1; <50 = 2
Fibrinogen (g/L)>1.0 = 0; <1.0 = 1
PT (sec prolonged)<3 = 0; 3-6 = 1; >6 = 2
D-dimer / FDPNo increase = 0; Moderate = 2; Strong = 3

Interpretation: ≥5 = Overt DIC; <5 = Non-overt (SIC) – monitor

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP: ISTH Score ≥5 = Overt DIC. Order: Platelets, Fibrinogen, PT, D-dimer. DIC = Consumption (↓Plt, ↓Fib, ↑PT, ↑D-dimer). Treat underlying cause FIRST.

🩺 Common Triggers

CategoryExamples
SepsisGram-negative (E. coli, Pseudomonas), Gram-positive, Meningococcal, Fungal – most common
ObstetricAbruptio placentae, Amniotic fluid embolism, Retained dead fetus, HELLP, Postpartum haemorrhage
Trauma/BurnsMajor trauma, crush injury, severe burns, neurotrauma
MalignancyAcute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML), Adenocarcinomas (pancreas, lung, prostate), Metastatic disease
VascularGiant haemangioma (Kasabach-Merritt), Aortic aneurysm
TransplantSolid organ, HSCT (engraftment syndrome, VOD/SOS)
OtherHeat stroke, Snake bite, Severe liver disease, Haemolysis

💊 Management

1. Treat the Underlying Cause – MOST IMPORTANT

TriggerSpecific Treatment
SepsisBroad-spectrum antibiotics + Source control
ObstetricDelivery (abruption), Uterine evacuation (retained products), Supportive for AFE
APMLATRA + ATO (reverses DIC rapidly)
MalignancyChemotherapy / Tumour resection
TraumaSurgical control, Massive transfusion protocol

2. Replacement Therapy – Only if Bleeding or Invasive Procedure

ComponentThreshold for ReplacementDose
Platelets<50 (prophylactic); <100 (CNS bleed/surgery)1 ATD / 10 kg
Fibrinogen<1.5 g/L (bleeding); <1.0 (ISTH score)Cryoprecipitate 1 pool/10 kg or Fibrinogen concentrate 25-50 mg/kg
Coagulation FactorsPT >1.5x normal (bleeding/surgery)FFP 15-20 mL/kg
RBCsHb <7-8 (symptomatic)Standard
[!warning] NO ROUTINE PROPHYLACTIC REPLACEMENT in non-bleeding DIC. Replacement only for active bleeding or before invasive procedures.

3. Anticoagulation – CONTROVERSIAL / GENERALLY AVOIDED

ScenarioRecommendation
Overt DIC with bleedingNO Heparin
Overt DIC with thrombosis (purpura fulminans, VTE)Therapeutic anticoagulation (LMWH/UFH) + Replacement
Chronic DIC / Thrombosis-predominantConsider LMWH (if no bleeding)
Purpura Fulminans (Meningococcal)Protein C concentrate + Heparin + FFP
[!warning] DIC ≠ Heparin prophylaxis. Therapeutic heparin ONLY if thrombosis dominates (purpura fulminans, VTE) AND no active bleeding.

4. Specific Scenarios

ScenarioKey Points
APMLATRA + ATO reverses DIC; Monitor fibrinogen, platelets; Avoid heparin
Obstetric DICDelivery is definitive; Replace fibrinogen early (<2 g/L); Cell salvage if massive
Purpura FulminansProtein C concentrate (if available) + Heparin + FFP; Limb ischaemia → fasciotomy

🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
TTPADAMTS13 <10%, Schistocytes, Normal Fibrinogen/PT, No underlying sepsis/trauma
HITHeparin 5-14d, PF4/heparin Ab+, Thrombosis, Normal PT/Fibrinogen
Liver DiseaseProlonged PT, Low Fibrinogen, Factor V LOW (DIC = FV normal), No D-dimer spike
Vitamin K DeficiencyPT ↑↑, PTT ↑, Normal Fibrinogen/Platelets/D-dimer, Responds to Vit K
Massive TransfusionDilutional coagulopathy; Improves with replacement; No D-dimer spike initially
Anticoagulant OverdoseHistory, Specific factor pattern (Warfarin = II,VII,IX,X; DOAC = specific assays)

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionSystemic coagulation activation → Consumption + Fibrinolysis
ISTH ScorePlatelets, Fibrinogen, PT, D-dimer → ≥5 = Overt DIC
Commonest CauseSepsis (Gram-negative)
ObstetricAbruption, AFE, Retained products
APMLATRA + ATO reverses DIC
Lab PatternPlt↓, Fibrinogen↓, PT↑, D-dimer↑↑
Management1. TREAT UNDERLYING CAUSE; 2. Replace if bleeding/procedure; 3. NO routine heparin
Replacement ThresholdsPlt <50, Fib <1.5, PT >1.5x (bleeding/surgery)
HeparinOnly if thrombosis dominant (purpura fulminans) + no bleeding
Liver vs DICLiver: Factor V LOW; DIC: Factor V NORMAL

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the ISTH Overt DIC scoring system?
  • Platelets (>100=0, 50-100=1, <50=2), Fibrinogen (>1=0, <1=1), PT (<3s=0, 3-6=1, >6=2), D-dimer (none=0, moderate=2, strong=3). ≥5 = Overt DIC
  1. What is the most common cause of DIC?
  • Sepsis (especially Gram-negative)
  1. How do you manage DIC?
  • 1. Treat underlying cause (antibiotics, delivery, ATRA for APML); 2. Replace factors/platelets ONLY if active bleeding or invasive procedure; 3. Avoid routine heparin
  1. What are the replacement thresholds in DIC?
  • Platelets <50 (prophylactic), <100 (CNS/surgery); Fibrinogen <1.5 g/L (bleeding); PT >1.5x normal → FFP
  1. When is heparin indicated in DIC?
  • Thrombosis-dominant DIC (purpura fulminans, VTE) WITHOUT active bleeding; Protein C concentrate + Heparin for purpura fulminans
  1. How does DIC differ from TTP?
  • DIC: PT↑, Fibrinogen↓, D-dimer↑↑, underlying trigger; TTP: ADAMTS13<10%, normal PT/Fibrinogen, schistocytes
  1. How does DIC differ from liver disease coagulopathy?
  • Liver: Factor V LOW; DIC: Factor V NORMAL (consumed equally but liver can't synthesise)
  1. What is the obstetric management of DIC?
  • Delivery is definitive (abruption); Replace fibrinogen early (<2 g/L); Massive transfusion protocol; Cell salvage
  1. How does APML-associated DIC differ?
  • Rapidly reversible with ATRA + ATO; Monitor fibrinogen closely; Avoid heparin
  1. What is purpura fulminans and its treatment?
  • Severe thrombotic DIC (meningococcal); Protein C concentrate + Heparin + FFP; Limb ischaemia → fasciotomy

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DIC vs TTPDIC = PT↑, Fib↓, D-dimer↑↑, trigger; TTP = ADAMTS13<10%, normal PT/Fib
DIC vs Liver DiseaseLiver = Factor V LOW; DIC = Factor V NORMAL
DIC vs HITHIT = Heparin + PF4 Ab + thrombosis; DIC = Trigger + consumption
Heparin in DICNO routine prophylaxis; Only therapeutic if thrombosis dominant + no bleeding
ReplacementOnly if BLEEDING or PROCEDURE; NOT prophylactic for lab numbers
MnemonicMeaning
"DIC = PT↑, Plt↓, Fib↓, D-dimer↑↑"Lab pattern
"ISTH ≥5 = Overt DIC"Score threshold
"Treat Cause First"Management priority
"Bleed = Replace; No Bleed = Watch"Replacement rule
"APML = ATRA Fixes DIC"APML-specific
"Liver = Low Factor V"Differentiation
"Purpura Fulminans = Protein C + Heparin"Specific treatment

🗺️ Mind Map

Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
Pathophysiology
TF Exposure → Thrombin → Fibrin
Consumption: Plts, Factors
Fibrinolysis: D-dimer↑
Triggers
Sepsis (Commonest)
Obstetric: Abruption, AFE
Trauma, Burns
APML, Malignancy
Vascular anomalies
Diagnosis
ISTH Score: Plt, Fib, PT, D-dimer
≥5 = Overt DIC
Management
1. TREAT CAUSE
2. Replace if Bleeding/Procedure
3. NO Routine Heparin
Replacement
Plt <50 (Prophyl), <100 (CNS/Surg)
Fib <1.5 (Bleed) → Cryo
PT >1.5x → FFP
Special
APML: ATRA/ATO
Obstetric: Delivery + Fibrinogen
Purpura Fulminans: Protein C + Heparin

📋 One-Page Revision Card

DIC – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
Definition: Systemic coagulation activation → Consumption + Fibrinolysis
ISTH Score: Plt, Fib, PT, D-dimer → ≥5 = Overt DIC
Commonest Cause: Sepsis
Obstetric: Abruption, AFE, Retained products
APML: ATRA + ATO reverses DIC
Labs: Plt↓, Fib↓, PT↑, D-dimer↑↑
Management: 1. Treat Cause; 2. Replace if Bleeding/Procedure; 3. NO Routine Heparin
Replace: Plt <50, Fib <1.5, PT >1.5x (bleed/surg)
Heparin: Only if Thrombosis dominant + No bleed (Purpura Fulminans)
Liver vs DIC: Liver = Low Factor V; DIC = Normal Factor V

ReviewDateScore (1-5)Next Review

🎯 Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

LevelContent
Must KnowISTH score, common triggers, lab pattern (Plt↓ Fib↓ PT↑ D-dimer↑↑), treat underlying cause first, replacement only for bleeding/procedure, NO routine heparin, APML/obstetric specifics, DIC vs TTP vs liver disease
Should KnowChronic vs acute DIC, purpura fulminans management (Protein C + heparin), non-overt DIC/SIC, fibrinogen concentrate vs cryoprecipitate, viscoelastic testing (ROTEM/TEG), anti-fibrinolytics (TXA) contraindicated in DIC, ISTH vs JAAM vs BC scores
Nice to KnowDIC in specific malignancies (APML, adenocarcinomas), endothelial biomarkers (TM, syndecan-1), recombinant thrombomodulin (articulate - Japan), antithrombin concentrate, tissue factor pathway inhibitor (TFPI), DIC in VOD/SOS, paediatric DIC (Kasabach-Merritt, meningococcal)

topics.title: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia All
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 25 Alignment: Haematological Malignancies → Acute Leukaemias → ALL
FCPS/MRCP Focus: B-ALL vs T-ALL immunophenotype, Ph+ ALL management, risk stratification (NCI/Rome), CNS prophylaxis, blinatumomab/inotuzumab/CAR-T

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define ALL: ≥20% lymphoblasts in BM (or defining genetic abnormalities)
  • [ ] Classify per WHO 2022/ICC: B-ALL (Ph+, Ph-like, iAMP21, DUX4, ZNF384, MEF2D) vs T-ALL
  • [ ] Apply NCI/Rome risk stratification (Standard vs High vs Very High)
  • [ ] Diagnose: CBC, BM aspirate/biopsy, flow cytometry (TdT, CD19, CD10, CD20, CD34, CD3, CD7, CD1a), cytogenetics (karyotype, FISH), NGS panel
  • [ ] Manage induction: UKALL/COG protocols (Vincristine, Dexamethasone, L-asparaginase, Doxorubicin, Cyclophosphamide)
  • [ ] Manage Ph+ ALL: TKI (Dasatinib/Ponatinib) + Chemo → HSCT
  • [ ] CNS prophylaxis: Intrathecal MTX/Ara-C ± Cranial Irradiation (high risk)
  • [ ] Consolidation/Interim maintenance/Delayed intensification/Maintenance (2-3 years)
  • [ ] Role of Blinatumomab (CD19/CD3 BiTE), Inotuzumab (CD22 ADC), CAR-T (tisagenlecleucel) in MRD+/relapsed
  • [ ] Monitor MRD: Flow (10⁻⁴), NGS (Ig/TCR clonotype, 10⁻⁵-10⁻⁶)
  • [ ] Understand Asparaginase hypersensitivity, thrombosis, pancreatitis management

📖 Definition & Classification

WHO 2022 / ICC Classification

CategorySubtypesKey Features
B-ALLPh+ (BCR::ABL1)t(9;22), poor prognosis historically, TKI changed outcomes
Ph-like (BCR-ABL1-like)Similar expression to Ph+, kinase-activating fusions (CRLF2, ABL1, ABL2, JAK2, EPOR) – TKI targetable
iAMP21Intra-chromosomal amp of chr21 – high risk
KMT2A-rearrangedt(4;11), t(11;19) – infant ALL, poor prognosis
ETV6::RUNX1t(12;21) – favourable, 25% paediatric B-ALL
Hyperdiploidy51-65 chromosomes – favourable
Hypodiploidy<44 chromosomes – adverse
DUX4, ZNF384, MEF2D, NUTM1-rearrangedEmerging entities
T-ALLTAL1, TLX1, TLX3, HOXA, LMO2 rearrangementsNOTCH1/FBXW7 mutations common; distinct protocol
[!tip] FCPS/MRCP: B-ALL = TdT+, CD19+, CD10+, CD34+; T-ALL = TdT+, CD7+, CD3+ (cytoplasmic), CD1a+ (cortical). Ph+ and Ph-like = TKI + chemo → HSCT. ETV6::RUNX1 & Hyperdiploidy = favourable.

⚙️ Pathophysiology

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Lymphoid Progenitor Mutations
↓
& C
Class II: Differentiation Block
↓
Leukaemic Stem Cell
BCR::ABL1 (Ph+), RAS, JAK2, CRLF2, IL7R
ETV6::RUNX1, TCF3::PBX1, PAX5, IKZF1, KMT2A-r
↓
Clonal Expansion → ≥20% Blasts
↓
Marrow Failure + Extramedullary (CNS, Testes, Nodes)

🔬 Diagnostic Workup

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected ALL: Blasts on PB or Pancytopenia
↓
CBC + Film: Lymphoblasts
↓
BM Aspirate + Biopsy
↓
Flow Cytometry: B-ALL vs T-ALL Panel
Cytogenetics: Karyotype + FISH Panel
Molecular: NGS Panel + RT-PCR for Fusions
↓
& H
Ph/Ph-like/iAMP21/KMT2A/ETV6::RUNX1/Hyperdiploid
↓
Risk Stratification: NCI/Rome + Genetics + MRD
↓
CNS Assessment: LP + CSF Flow/Cytology
Baseline: Echo, LFT, Renal, Coag, TPMT, Viral Screen, HLA Typing

Flow Cytometry – Key Markers

LineagePositive MarkersNegative/Variable
B-ALLTdT+, CD19+, CD10+, CD34+, CD22+, CD79a+, HLA-DR+CD20 (variable, ~50%), CD13/CD33 (myeloid markers ~20%)
T-ALLTdT+, CD7+, cCD3+, CD2+, CD5+, CD1a+ (cortical)CD19-, CD10-, MPO-
Early Pre-BTdT+, CD19+, CD10-, CD34+, HLA-DR+
Pro-BTdT+, CD19+, CD10-, CD34-, CD38+, HLA-DR+

Essential Genetic Panel

TestTargetMethod
BCR::ABL1 (p190)Ph+ ALLRT-PCR (quantitative), FISH
KMT2A (11q23)Infant ALL, t(4;11) etc.FISH, RT-PCR
ETV6::RUNX1t(12;21) – favourableFISH, RT-PCR
Hyperdiploidy51-65 chromosomesKaryotype, SNP array
iAMP21chr21 amplificationFISH (RUNX1 probe)
Ph-like panelCRLF2, ABL1/2, JAK2, EPOR fusionsRNA-seq, targeted NGS
IKZF1 deletionPoor prognosis markerMLPA, NGS
NOTCH1/FBXW7T-ALL mutationsNGS

📊 Risk Stratification (NCI/Rome + Modified by Genetics/MRD)

NCI/Rome Clinical Risk (Age & WBC)

Risk GroupAgeWBC (×10⁹/L)
Standard Risk (SR)1-9.99 years<50
High Risk (HR)≥10 yearsOR ≥50

Integrated Risk (Genetics + Early Response)

Final RiskCriteria
Low/StandardSR clinical + Favourable genetics (ETV6::RUNX1, Hyperdiploidy) + Day 15/29 MRD negative
IntermediateHR clinical OR Unfavourable genetics OR MRD positive at end-induction
High/Very HighPh+ / Ph-like / iAMP21 / KMT2A-r / Hypodiploidy OR MRD >10⁻³ at end-induction OR Induction failure
[!warning] MRD is the strongest prognostic factor: End-induction MRD ≥10⁻³ = Very High Risk

💊 Management – Paediatric-Inspired Protocols (UKALL/COG/EORTC)

Induction (4-5 weeks) – Goal: CR + MRD negative

DrugDose/ScheduleKey Toxicity
Vincristine1.5 mg/m² (max 2mg) weekly × 4Neuropathy, ileus, SIADH
Dexamethasone6-10 mg/m²/day days 1-14, 15-21Hyperglycaemia, mood, osteonecrosis, infection
L-AsparaginasePEG-Asp 2500 IU/m² days 3, 10, 17 (or Erwinia if allergic)Hypersensitivity, thrombosis, pancreatitis, coagulopathy
Doxorubicin25-30 mg/m² days 1, 2, 3 (or 1, 8, 15)Cardiotoxicity, mucositis
Cyclophosphamide1 g/m² day 15 (or day 29)Haemorrhagic cystitis (mesna), infertility
Intrathecal MTXAge-based (6-15mg) days 1, 8, 15, 22Neurotoxicity
[!tip] FCPS/MRCP: Dexamethasone preferred over Prednisolone (better CNS penetration). PEG-Asparaginase standard; monitor fibrinogen, triglycerides, pancreatic enzymes. TPMT genotyping before 6-MP.

Ph+ ALL (BCR::ABL1) – Specific Protocol

  1. Induction: Multi-agent chemo + TKI (Dasatinib 60-100mg/day OR Ponatinib 30-45mg/day)
  2. Consolidation: Chemo + TKI
  3. Allo-HSCT in CR1 (preferred if donor) – TKI continued peri-transplant
  4. Post-HSCT: TKI maintenance (Dasatinib/Ponatinib)

Ph-like ALL

  • TKI + Chemo if ABL-class fusion (ABL1/2, PDGFR, etc.) → Dasatinib
  • Ruxolitinib if JAK-STAT pathway (CRLF2/JAK2/EPOR)
  • HSCT in CR1 if MRD+ or high risk

T-ALL

  • NELL-1/COG/UKALL protocols: Higher dose methotrexate, nelarabine (relapsed)
  • NOTCH1/FBXW7 mut = favourable in some protocols
  • CNS prophylaxis critical (higher CNS relapse risk)

🧠 CNS Prophylaxis

Risk GroupProphylaxis
Standard RiskIntrathecal MTX × 4-6 during induction/consolidation + High-dose MTX (consolidation)
High RiskIT MTX + High-dose MTX + Cranial Irradiation (12-18Gy) (controversial, decreasing use)
CNS2 (WBC<5 but blasts+)Intensified IT (biweekly × 6) + HD-MTX ± Cranial RT
CNS3 (WBC≥5 or clinical)Cranial RT (18-24Gy) + Intensified IT + HD-MTX ± Spinal RT
[!warning] CNS3 = cranial irradiation (historically 24Gy, now 18Gy). CNS2 = intensified IT without RT if possible.

🔬 MRD Monitoring – Critical for Risk Stratification

TimepointMethodThresholdAction
Day 15 (Early)Flow (10⁻⁴)>10⁻³ (UKALL)Intensify
Day 29 (End-Induction)Flow (10⁻⁴) + NGS (10⁻⁵-10⁻⁶)<10⁻⁴ = MRD negativeStandard risk
≥10⁻⁴ = MRD positiveHigh risk → HSCT consideration
Post-ConsolidationNGS preferred≥10⁻⁴Pre-HSCT intervention (blinatumomab)
Post-HSCTq3mo × 2yrRising/positivePre-emptive DLI/blinatumomab

MRD Methods

  • Flow: LAIP (Leukaemia-Associated Immunophenotype) or Different-from-Normal – sensitivity 10⁻⁴
  • NGS (Ig/TCR clonotype): Patient-specific primers – sensitivity 10⁻⁵-10⁻⁶ (gold standard)
  • RT-qPCR: Fusion transcripts (ETV6::RUNX1, BCR::ABL1) – sensitivity 10⁻⁴-10⁻⁵

💊 Consolidation / Maintenance (Total 2-3 years)

PhaseDurationKey Components
Consolidation2-3 cyclesHD-MTX (5 g/m²), 6-MP, Ara-C, Vincristine, Dex
Interim Maintenance8-9 weeksMTX (weekly), 6-MP, Vincristine, IT MTX
Delayed Intensification8 weeksRe-induction style: VCR, Dex, PEG-Asp, Doxo, Cyc, Ara-C, 6-MP, IT
Maintenance2 years (girls) / 3 years (boys) from CRDaily 6-MP + Weekly MTX + Monthly VCR/Dex pulses + IT MTX q12wk
[!tip] FCPS/MRCP: Maintenance = 6-MP daily + MTX weekly + VCR/Dex monthly pulses. TPMT genotype guides 6-MP dose. Duration: 2y girls, 3y boys (testicular relapse risk).

⚠️ Key Complications & Specific Management

ComplicationManagement
Asparaginase HypersensitivitySwitch to Erwinia asparaginase (25,000 IU/m² MWF × 6-9 doses) or pegcrisaspase
Thrombosis (Asparaginase-related)ATIII replacement if <80%; LMWH prophylaxis in high risk; therapeutic anticoagulation if thrombosis
PancreatitisStop asparaginase; supportive; may reintroduce Erwinia after resolution
Osteonecrosis (AVN)Bisphosphonates, analgesic, orthopaedic referral; risk: Dex, age>10, female
HyperglycaemiaInsulin sliding scale; monitor during Dex pulses
TLSAllopurinol/rasburicase, hydration (as per AML)
Veno-occlusive Disease (VOD/SOS)Defibrotide prophylaxis in high-risk HSCT

🌱 Relapsed/Refractory ALL – Modern Immunotherapy

AgentMechanismIndicationKey Points
BlinatumomabCD19/CD3 BiTE (T-cell engager)MRD+ (≥10⁻⁴), Relapsed/Refractory B-ALLIVCI 28d cycles, step-dosing (9→28µg/d), neurotoxicity (ICANS), CRS, dexamethasone premed
Inotuzumab OzogamicinAnti-CD22 ADC (calicheamicin)Relapsed/Refractory B-ALL, salvageFractionated dosing (0.8/0.5/0.5 mg/m²), VOD/SOS risk, myelosuppression
Tisagenlecleucel (CAR-T)CD19 CAR-TR/R B-ALL ≤25y, ≥2 linesELIANA trial, CRS (tocilizumab), neurotoxicity, B-cell aplasia → IVIG
TKI (Dasatinib/Ponatinib)BCR-ABL1 inhibitorPh+ ALL, Ph-like (ABL-class)Combined with chemo; post-HSCT maintenance
NelarabinePurine analogueT-ALL relapseNeurotoxicity (peripheral/central)
[!tip] FCPS/MRCP: Blinatumomab = MRD+ B-ALL bridge to HSCT. Inotuzumab = salvage, watch VOD. CAR-T = R/R ≤25y. TKI + chemo = Ph+ ALL → HSCT CR1.

🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
AMLMPO+, CD117+, CD13/33+, TdT- (usually); Auer rods
Lymphoblastic LymphomaMass lesion ± BM <25% blasts; same immunophenotype/genetics as ALL; treated as ALL
Burkitt Lymphoma/LeukaemiasIgM+, CD10+, BCL2-, MYC translocation (t(8;14)), mature B-cells, Ki-67 100%
Mature B-ALL (L3)FAL morphology, sIg+, MYC-r – actually Burkitt; treated with BL protocols
ETV6::RUNX1 ALLFavourable, older child, low WBC, hyperdiploid often co-exists

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
ALL Definition≥20% lymphoblasts (TdT+) in BM
B-ALL ImmunophenotypeTdT+, CD19+, CD10+, CD34+, HLA-DR+
T-ALL ImmunophenotypeTdT+, CD7+, cCD3+, CD1a+ (cortical)
Ph+ ALLBCR::ABL1 p190; TKI (Dasatinib/Ponatinib) + Chemo → HSCT CR1
Ph-like ALLABL-class → Dasatinib; JAK-STAT → Ruxolitinib
Favourable GeneticsETV6::RUNX1 (t(12;21)), Hyperdiploidy (51-65 chr)
Adverse GeneticsPh+, Ph-like, iAMP21, KMT2A-r, Hypodiploidy (<44), IKZF1 del
Risk StratificationNCI/Rome (Age/WBC) + Genetics + MRD
MRDFlow 10⁻⁴, NGS 10⁻⁵-10⁻⁶ – strongest prognostic factor
CNS ProphylaxisIT MTX + HD-MTX; CNS3 = Cranial RT (18Gy)
Maintenance6-MP daily + MTX weekly + VCR/Dex monthly × 2y (F) / 3y (M)
Asparaginase ToxicityHypersensitivity → Erwinia; Thrombosis → ATIII/LMWH; Pancreatitis → Stop
Relapsed B-ALLBlinatumomab (MRD+) / Inotuzumab (salvage) / CAR-T (R/R ≤25y)

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the immunophenotype of B-ALL vs T-ALL?
  • B-ALL: TdT+, CD19+, CD10+, CD34+, HLA-DR+; T-ALL: TdT+, CD7+, cCD3+, CD1a+, CD5+
  1. How is Ph+ ALL managed differently from Ph-negative ALL?
  • TKI (Dasatinib/Ponatinib) + Chemo induction → Allo-HSCT in CR1 ± TKI maintenance
  1. What is Ph-like ALL and how is it treated?
  • BCR-ABL1-like expression without BCR::ABL1; kinase fusions (CRLF2, ABL1/2, JAK2) → TKI (Dasatinib) if ABL-class, Ruxolitinib if JAK-STAT + chemo
  1. What are the favourable and adverse genetic features in ALL?
  • Favourable: ETV6::RUNX1, Hyperdiploidy; Adverse: Ph+, Ph-like, iAMP21, KMT2A-r, Hypodiploidy, IKZF1 del
  1. Describe NCI/Rome risk stratification.
  • Standard Risk: Age 1-9.99y AND WBC<50; High Risk: Age≥10y OR WBC≥50
  1. When is cranial irradiation indicated in ALL?
  • CNS3 (WBC≥5/µL in CSF or clinical CNS disease) – 18Gy; CNS2 = intensified IT without RT (preferred)
  1. What is the maintenance regimen duration for ALL?
  • 6-MP daily + MTX weekly + VCR/Dex monthly pulses; 2 years girls, 3 years boys
  1. How do you monitor MRD in ALL and what is the significant threshold?
  • Flow (10⁻⁴) and NGS (Ig/TCR, 10⁻⁵-10⁻⁶); End-induction MRD ≥10⁻⁴ = positive = high risk
  1. What are the major toxicities of L-asparaginase and their management?
  • Hypersensitivity → Erwinia/Pegcrisaspase; Thrombosis → ATIII replacement + LMWH; Pancreatitis → Stop asparaginase
  1. What is the role of blinatumomab in ALL?
  • CD19/CD3 BiTE; indicated for MRD+ B-ALL (≥10⁻⁴) and R/R B-ALL; bridge to HSCT; neurotoxicity (ICANS/CRS)

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
ALL vs AML flowALL = TdT+; AML = MPO+
Ph+ vs Ph-likePh+ = BCR::ABL1; Ph-like = similar signature, other kinase fusions (targetable)
ETV6::RUNX1 vs BCR::ABL1ETV6::RUNX1 = favourable; BCR::ABL1 = adverse (without TKI)
CNS2 vs CNS3CNS2: WBC<5 with blasts → IT intensification; CNS3: WBC≥5 → Cranial RT
Maintenance durationGirls 2 years, Boys 3 years (testicular sanctuary site)
MnemonicMeaning
"ALL = TdT+, AML = MPO+"Lineage-defining markers
"Ph+ = TKI + HSCT"Ph+ ALL management
"ETV6 = Excellent Prognosis"t(12;21) favourable
"iAMP21 = Awful Prognosis"Adverse genetics
"MRD = Strongest Prognosis"End-induction MRD guides risk
"CNS3 = Cranium (RT)"Cranial irradiation for CNS3
"Maintenance: 2F, 3M"Duration by sex

🗺️ Mind Map

Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia
Definition
≥20% lymphoblasts (TdT+)
Classification
B-ALL: TdT, CD19, CD10, CD34
T-ALL: TdT, CD7, cCD3, CD1a
Ph+ (BCR::ABL1 p190)
Ph-like (kinase fusions)
ETV6::RUNX1 (favourable)
Hyperdiploidy (favourable)
KMT2A-r, iAMP21, Hypodiploidy (adverse)
Risk Stratification
NCI/Rome: Age + WBC
Genetics + MRD
Induction
VCR + Dex + PEG-Asp + Doxo + Cyc
Ph+: + TKI (Dasatinib/Ponatinib)
Ph-like: + Dasatinib/Ruxolitinib
CNS Prophylaxis
IT MTX + HD-MTX
CNS3 = Cranial RT 18Gy
MRD
Flow 10⁻⁴, NGS 10⁻⁵-10⁻⁶
End-induction ≥10⁻⁴ = High Risk
Maintenance
6-MP + MTX + VCR/Dex pulses
2y F / 3y M
Asparaginase Toxicity
Hypersensitivity → Erwinia
Thrombosis → ATIII/LMWH
Pancreatitis → Stop
Relapse
Blinatumomab (MRD+)
Inotuzumab (salvage)
CAR-T (R/R ≤25y)

📋 One-Page Revision Card

ALL – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
B-ALL: TdT+, CD19+, CD10+, CD34+, HLA-DR+
T-ALL: TdT+, CD7+, cCD3+, CD1a+, CD5+
Ph+: BCR::ABL1 p190 → TKI (Dasatinib/Ponatinib) + Chemo → HSCT CR1
Ph-like: Kinase fusions → ABL-class = Dasatinib; JAK-STAT = Ruxolitinib
Favourable: ETV6::RUNX1 (t12;21), Hyperdiploidy (51-65 chr)
Adverse: Ph+, Ph-like, iAMP21, KMT2A-r, Hypodiploidy (<44), IKZF1 del
Risk: NCI/Rome (Age/WBC) + Genetics + MRD (strongest)
Induction: VCR + Dex + PEG-Asp + Doxo + Cyc (+ TKI if Ph+)
CNS: IT MTX + HD-MTX; CNS3 = RT 18Gy
MRD: Flow 10⁻⁴, NGS 10⁻⁵; ≥10⁻⁴ at end-induction = High Risk
Maintenance: 6-MP daily + MTX weekly + VCR/Dex monthly 2y F / 3y M
Asp Toxicity: Hypersensitivity→Erwinia; Thrombosis→ATIII/LMWH; Pancreatitis→Stop
Relapse: Blinatumomab (MRD+), Inotuzumab (salvage), CAR-T (R/R ≤25y)

topics.title: Doac Management
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 25 Alignment: Bleeding and Thrombotic Disorders → Anticoagulation → DOACs
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Dabigatran, Edoxaban; Indications, Dosing, Renal/Hepatic Adjustment, Reversal, Monitoring, Switching

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define DOACs: Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Dabigatran, Edoxaban - Mechanism, Pharmacokinetics
  • [ ] Apply Indications: AF (NVAF), VTE Treatment/Prevention, Post-orthopaedic Surgery
  • [ ] Apply Dosing: Standard, Renal Impairment, Elderly, Low Body Weight
  • [ ] Apply Renal/Hepatic Adjustment: eGFR Cutoffs, Hepatic Impairment
  • [ ] Manage Reversal: Andexanet Alfa (Factor Xa Inhibitors), Idarucizumab (Dabigatran), PCC
  • [ ] Manage Peri-procedural: Hold/Restart Timing, Bridging (Not Routine)
  • [ ] Manage Switching: Warfarin ↔ DOAC, DOAC ↔ DOAC
  • [ ] Manage Bleeding: Minor/Major, Reversal Agents, Supportive Care
  • [ ] Manage Special Populations: Elderly, Renal Impairment, Obesity, Pregnancy, Cancer

📖 DOAC Comparison

FeatureRivaroxabanApixabanDabigatranEdoxaban
MechanismDirect Factor Xa InhibitorDirect Factor Xa InhibitorDirect Thrombin (IIa) InhibitorDirect Factor Xa Inhibitor
Bioavailability~66% (With Food)~50%~6.5% (Prodrug)~62%
Half-life5-9 h (Young), 11-13 h (Elderly)12 h12-17 h10-14 h
Renal Clearance66%27%80%50%
Hepatic MetabolismCYP3A4, CYP2J2CYP3A4Minimal (Esterase)CYP3A4
P-gp SubstrateYesYesYesYes
Food Effect↑ Absorption (With Food)MinimalFood ↑ Absorption 2xMinimal

💊 Dosing Regimens

Atrial Fibrillation (Stroke Prevention)

AgentStandard DoseRenal Impairment (CrCl)Low Weight / Elderly
Rivaroxaban20 mg OD (With Evening Meal)15 mg OD if CrCl 15-49 mL/min15 mg OD if Weight ≤60 kg or Age ≥80
Apixaban5 mg BD2.5 mg BD if ≥2 Criteria: Age ≥80, Weight ≤60 kg, CrCl ≤1.5 mg/dL2.5 mg BD if ≥2 Criteria
Dabigatran150 mg BD (110 mg BD if Age ≥80 or High Bleed Risk)110 mg BD if CrCl 30-49 mL/min; Contraindicated <30110 mg BD if Age ≥80
Edoxaban60 mg OD30 mg OD if CrCl 15-50, Weight ≤60 kg, or Concomitant P-gp Inhibitors30 mg OD if ≥1 Criterion

VTE Treatment (DVT/PE)

AgentAcute PhaseMaintenance
Rivaroxaban15 mg BD × 21 days (With Food)20 mg OD (With Evening Meal)
Apixaban10 mg BD × 7 days5 mg BD
DabigatranLMWH 5 days Overlap → 150 mg BD (110 mg BD if High Bleed Risk)Same
EdoxabanLMWH 5 days Overlap → 60 mg OD (30 mg OD if Adjusted)Same

Post-Orthopaedic Surgery Prophylaxis

SurgeryAgent & DoseDuration
Hip ReplacementRivaroxaban 10 mg OD / Apixaban 2.5 mg BD / Dabigatran 220 mg OD35 days
Knee ReplacementRivaroxaban 10 mg OD / Apixaban 2.5 mg BD / Dabigatran 150 mg OD10-14 days

🩸 Renal & Hepatic Adjustment Summary

AgentCrCl ≥50CrCl 30-49CrCl 15-29CrCl <15 / DialysisHepatic Impairment
RivaroxabanStandard15 mg OD (AF) / 15 mg BD→20mg (VTE)Avoid (AF) / Avoid (VTE)AvoidChild-Pugh C: Avoid
ApixabanStandardStandard2.5 mg BDAvoid (Off-label 2.5mg BD on Dialysis)Child-Pugh C: Avoid
DabigatranStandard110 mg BDContraindicatedContraindicatedChild-Pugh B/C: Avoid
EdoxabanStandard30 mg OD30 mg ODContraindicatedChild-Pugh B/C: Avoid
[!warning] Renal Adjustment: Apixaban = Safest in CKD/Dialysis; Dabigatran = Contraindicated <30; Rivaroxaban/Edoxaban = Avoid <15.

🔄 Reversal Agents

AgentReversal AgentDoseIndication
DabigatranIdarucizumab (Praxbind)5 g IV (2 × 2.5 g vials, 5 min apart)Life-threatening Bleeding / Emergency Surgery
Rivaroxaban / Apixaban / EdoxabanAndexanet Alfa (Ondexxya)Bolus + 2h Infusion (Low/High Dose per Agent)Life-threatening Bleeding / Emergency Surgery
All DOACs4-Factor PCC (Kcentra)25-50 IU/kgIf Specific Reversal Unavailable
All DOACsActivated Charcoal50 g PORecent Ingestion (<2-4h)
Reversal AgentDosing Details
Idarucizumab5 g IV (2 × 2.5 g/50mL) over 5-10 min; Repeat 2.5g if Rebound
Andexanet Alfa (Low Dose)Rivaroxaban ≤10mg / Apixaban ≤5mg: 400mg Bolus + 480mg/2h Infusion
Andexanet Alfa (High Dose)Rivaroxaban >10mg / Apixaban >5mg / Edoxaban: 800mg Bolus + 960mg/2h Infusion
[!warning] Andexanet Alfa: Rebound Anticoagulation Risk → Restart Anticoagulation ASAP if Indicated. Idarucizumab: Only for Dabigatran.

🏥 Peri-procedural Management

Procedure Bleeding RiskDOAC HoldRestart
Low (Dental, Cataract, Superficial)Skip Morning Dose (If OD) / Skip 1 Dose (If BD)Next Scheduled Dose
Moderate (Endoscopy w/ Biopsy, Minor Surgery)Skip 24h (OD) / Skip 2 Doses (BD)6-24h Post-op (If Haemostasis Secure)
High (Major Surgery, Neurosurgery, Spinal, Cardiac)Skip 48h (OD) / Skip 4 Doses (48h) (BD)48-72h Post-op (If Haemostasis Secure)
Renal FunctionRivaroxaban/Edoxaban HoldApixaban HoldDabigatran Hold
Normal24h24h24-48h
CrCl 30-5024h24h48h
CrCl <30Contraindicated (Rivaroxaban/Edoxaban)48hContraindicated
[!warning] No Routine Bridging with LMWH for DOACs (Unlike Warfarin). Restart Based on Bleeding Risk not INR.

🩸 Bleeding Management

SeverityManagement
Minor (Epistaxis, Bruising)Hold 1-2 Doses, Local Measures, Restart when Stable
Moderate (Macroscopic Haematuria, GI Bleed Stable)Hold DOAC, IV Fluids, Transfuse if Hb <70, Consider Reversal
Major/Life-threatening (ICH, Massive GI Bleed, Haemodynamic Instability)STOP DOAC, Call Haematology, Specific Reversal Agent + PCC, Surgical/IR Intervention, ICU

🔄 Switching Anticoagulants

SwitchProtocol
Warfarin → DOACStop Warfarin, Start DOAC when INR <2.0 (Apixaban/Dabigatran) or INR <2.5 (Rivaroxaban/Edoxaban)
DOAC → WarfarinStart Warfarin + Continue DOAC until INR ≥2.0 for 2 Days, Then Stop DOAC
DOAC → DOACStop DOAC 1, Start DOAC 2 at Next Scheduled Dose
LMWH → DOACGive DOAC at Time of Next LMWH Dose
DOAC → LMWHGive LMWH at Time of Next DOAC Dose

🤰 DOACs in Special Populations

PopulationRecommendation
Elderly (≥80)Apixaban 2.5mg BD (Preferred), Rivaroxaban 15mg OD, Dabigatran 110mg BD, Edoxaban 30mg OD
Renal ImpairmentApixaban Preferred (2.5mg BD if CrCl 15-29); Avoid Dabigatran/Edoxaban/Rivaroxaban if CrCl<15
Obesity (BMI >40 / Weight >120kg)Standard Dose OK (Apixaban/Rivaroxaban); Avoid Dabigatran (Limited Data)
Cancer (VTE)LMWH Preferred (CLOT Trial); DOACs Non-Inferior (SELECT-D, CARAVAGGIO) - Apixaban Preferred
PregnancyCONTRAINDICATED (All DOACs) → LMWH Throughout
LactationAvoid (Limited Data) → LMWH Preferred
Mechanical Heart ValveCONTRAINDICATED (RE-ALIGN Trial Harm) → Warfarin Only
Antiphospholipid SyndromeDOACs Contraindicated in Triple Positive (TRAPS, ASTRO-APS) → Warfarin INR 3-4

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
MechanismsRiva/Apx/Edo = Factor Xa Inhibitors; Dabi = Thrombin (IIa) Inhibitor
Renal ClearanceDabi 80% > Edo 50% > Riva 66% > Apx 27% → Apx Safest in CKD
AF DosingRiva 20mg OD (15mg if CrCl 15-49); Apx 5mg BD (2.5mg if ≥2 Criteria); Dabi 150mg BD (110mg if ≥80/High Bleed); Edo 60mg OD (30mg if Criteria)
VTE LoadingRiva 15mg BD×21d → 20mg; Apx 10mg BD×7d → 5mg BD; Dabi 5d LMWH → 150mg BD
ReversalDabi = Idarucizumab 5g IV; Riva/Apx/Edo = Andexanet Alfa; All = PCC 4-Factor
Peri-proceduralLow Risk: Skip 1 Dose; High Risk: 48h Hold (Riva/Apx/Edo), 48-72h (Dabi)
Renal ImpairmentApx Safest (2.5mg BD down to CrCl 15); Dabi Contraindicated <30; Riva/Edo Avoid <15
Reversal AgentsIdarucizumab = Dabi Only (5g IV); Andexanet = Factor Xa Inhibitors
SwitchingWarfarin→DOAC: Start DOAC when INR<2.0 (Apx/Dabi) / <2.5 (Riva/Edo)
ContraindicationsMechanical Valve, Pregnancy, Triple Positive APS, Severe Hepatic (Child-Pugh C)

❓ Viva Questions

  1. Which DOAC is preferred in a patient with severe renal impairment (CrCl 20 mL/min)?
  • Apixaban 2.5 mg BD (Only DOAC licensed down to CrCl 15 mL/min)
  1. How do you reverse dabigatran in life-threatening bleeding?
  • Idarucizumab 5g IV (2 × 2.5g vials) over 5-10 minutes
  1. What is the antidote for rivaroxaban/apixaban/edoxaban?
  • Andexanet alfa (Factor Xa inhibitor antidote); 4-factor PCC if unavailable
  1. When should you hold DOACs before elective surgery?
  • Low Risk: Skip 1 dose; High Risk: 48h (Factor Xa) / 48-72h (Dabigatran)
  1. Can DOACs be used in mechanical heart valves?
  • NO - CONTRAINDICATED (RE-ALIGN trial showed excess thromboembolic events with dabigatran)
  1. How do you switch from warfarin to apixaban?
  • Stop warfarin, Start apixaban when INR <2.0
  1. Which DOAC is safest in dialysis patients?
  • Apixaban 2.5mg BD (Only DOAC with dialysis data; Off-label but Used)
  1. How do you manage peri-procedural anticoagulation for a patient on dabigatran 150mg BD for AF undergoing colonoscopy with polypectomy?
  • Hold Dabigatran 48h Pre-procedure (CrCl >50); Restart 6-24h Post-procedure if Haemostasis Secure
  1. What is the reversal agent for factor Xa inhibitors and its dosing?
  • Andexanet Alfa: Low Dose (Riva ≤10mg/Apx ≤5mg): 400mg Bolus + 480mg/2h Infusion; High Dose: 800mg Bolus + 960mg/2h Infusion
  1. When can you restart DOAC after major surgery?
  • 48-72h Post-op if Haemostasis Secure; Earlier for Low Bleed Risk
  1. Why is apixaban preferred over other DOACs in CKD?
  • Lowest Renal Clearance (27%), Licensed down to CrCl 15, Consistent Pharmacokinetics
  1. How do you switch from rivaroxaban to warfarin?
  • Start Warfarin + Continue Rivaroxaban until INR ≥2.0 for 2 consecutive days, Then Stop Rivaroxaban

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Renal Clearance RankingDabigatran (80%) > Edoxaban (50%) > Rivaroxaban (66%) > Apixaban (27%)
Rivaroxaban vs Apixaban DosingRiva = OD (With Food); Apx = BD (Food Independent)
Dabigatran vs OthersOnly Direct Thrombin Inhibitor; Only Idarucizumab Reversal; Contraindicated CrCl<30
EdoxabanRequires 5-day LMWH Lead-in for VTE; P-gp Inhibitors → Dose Reduce
Reversal AgentsIdarucizumab = Dabigatran ONLY; Andexanet = Factor Xa Inhibitors (Riva/Apx/Edo)
Switching Warfarin→DOACINR <2.0 (Apx/Dabi); INR <2.5 (Riva/Edo)
Mechanical ValveDOACs CONTRAINDICATED (RE-ALIGN)
MnemonicMeaning
"Riva = OD, Apx = BD, Dabi = BD, Edo = OD"Dosing Frequency
"Apx = Safest in CKD"Renal Safety
"Idarucizumab = Dabi ONLY"Reversal Specificity
"Andexanet = Xa Inhibitors (Riva/Apx/Edo)"Andexanet Scope
"DOACs = NO in Mech Valve / Pregnancy / Triple APS"Contraindications
"Switch Warfarin→DOAC: INR<2 (Apx/Dabi), <2.5 (Riva/Edo)"Switching Rule

topics.title: Dvt Pe Cancer Associated Thrombosis
topics.html:
[!info] Davidson Ch 25 Alignment: Bleeding and Thrombotic Disorders → Venous Thromboembolism
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Wells/PE/Geneva scores, D-dimer rule-out, DOACs first-line VTE, CAT: LMWH preferred (CLOT, SELECT-D, CARAVAGGIO), duration, IVC filter, massive PE thrombolysis

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Apply Clinical Probability Scores: Wells DVT, Wells PE, Geneva PE for pre-test probability
  • [ ] Use D-dimer with age-adjusted cut-off (Age × 10 μg/L if >50) to rule out low-probability VTE
  • [ ] Diagnose: Compression US (DVT), CTPA (PE), V/Q Scan (renal/contrast allergy)
  • [ ] Treat VTE: DOACs first-line (Rivaroxaban/Apixaban); Warfarin if mechanical valves/pregnancy/AT def
  • [ ] Manage Cancer-Associated Thrombosis (CAT): LMWH preferred (CLOT trial) – DOACs non-inferior but ↑ bleeding in GI/GU (SELECT-D, CARAVAGGIO)
  • [ ] Determine Duration: Provoked (3mo), Unprovoked (indefinite), CAT (indefinite while cancer active)
  • [ ] Manage Massive PE: Thrombolysis (Alteplase 100mg IV) if haemodynamically unstable; Surgical embolectomy if contraindicated
  • [ ] IVC Filter indications: Contraindication to anticoagulation, recurrent VTE on therapeutic anticoagulation

📖 VTE Diagnosis – Clinical Probability Scores

Wells DVT Score

FeaturePoints
Active cancer (treatment ≤6mo/palliative)1
Paralysis/paresis/immobilisation >3d1
Recently bedridden >3d / surgery ≤4wks1
Localised tenderness along deep veins1
Entire leg swollen1
Calf swelling >3cm (vs asymptomatic)1
Pitting oedema (symptomatic leg)1
Collateral superficial veins1
Alternative diagnosis as likely or greater-2
ScoreProbabilityAction
≤0Low (~5%)D-dimer (if neg → DVT excluded)
1-2Moderate (~17%)D-dimer (if neg → excluded) / US
≥3High (~75%)Direct to US (D-dimer not useful)

Wells PE Score

FeaturePoints
Clinical signs of DVT3
PE #1 diagnosis / equally likely3
HR >1001.5
Immobilisation >3d / surgery ≤4wks1.5
Previous DVT/PE1.5
Haemoptysis1
Cancer (treatment ≤6mo/palliative)1
Score (Traditional)ProbabilitySimplified (PE likely/unlikely)
>6HighPE Likely (>4)
2-6Moderate
<2LowPE Unlikely (≤4)

Geneva PE Score (Revised)

FeaturePoints
Age >651
Previous DVT/PE2
Surgery/Immobilisation ≤1mo2
Active cancer2
Unilateral leg pain3
Haemoptysis2
HR 75-94 / ≥953 / 5
Pain on leg palpation4

Low 0-3, Intermediate 4-10, High ≥11

🔬 D-dimer & Imaging

D-dimer Strategy

Pre-test ProbabilityD-dimer Cut-offAction if Negative
Low/UnlikelyAge-adjusted (Age × 10 μg/L if >50)VTE Excluded
Moderate/IntermediateStandard (500 μg/L)VTE Excluded
High/LikelyNOT usefulDirect to Imaging
[!tip] Age-adjusted D-dimer: Age × 10 μg/L for patients >50 years (increases specificity, maintains sensitivity)

Imaging

ConditionFirst-lineAlternative
DVTCompression US (proximal veins)Repeat US in 1 week if initial neg + high prob
PECTPAV/Q Scan (renal failure, contrast allergy, pregnancy)
PregnancyCompression US (if DVT symptoms) → V/Q or CTPA (low dose)

💊 VTE Treatment

Acute Phase (First 5-21 days)

DrugRegimen
Rivaroxaban15 mg BD × 21 days → 20 mg OD
Apixaban10 mg BD × 7 days → 5 mg BD
DabigatranHeparin/LMWH 5d overlap → 150 mg BD
EdoxabanHeparin/LMWH 5d overlap → 60 mg OD
WarfarinLMWH overlap ≥5d + INR 2-3
[!tip] DOACs = First-line for VTE (no monitoring, fixed dose, less bleeding than warfarin). Warfarin if: Mechanical valves, Pregnancy, AT Deficiency, APS (triple positive), Severe renal failure (CrCl <15-30).

Duration of Anticoagulation

ScenarioDuration
Provoked VTE (surgery, trauma, immobilisation, OCP, pregnancy)3 months
Unprovoked VTEIndefinite (if bleeding risk low; reassess annually)
Cancer-Associated (CAT)Indefinite while cancer active
Antiphospholipid SyndromeIndefinite (INR 3-4 if arterial/triple positive)
Recurrent VTEIndefinite
Protein C/S/AT DeficiencyIndefinite (if unprovoked)

🦠 Cancer-Associated Thrombosis (CAT)

Epidemiology & Risk

FactorVTE Risk
Cancer TypePancreas, Brain, Stomach, Lung, Ovary, Kidney, Lymphoma > Breast, Prostate
StageMetastatic > Localised
TreatmentChemotherapy, Surgery, Hormonal (Tamoxifen), Anti-angiogenic (Bevacizumab), Immunotherapy
BiomarkersD-dimer ↑, Platelets ↑, Leukocytosis, Low albumin, High CRP

Khorana Score (Risk Stratification for Prophylaxis)

VariablePoints
Site: Pancreas/Stomach2
Site: Lung/Lymphoma/Gynae/Bladder/Testis1
Pre-chemo Platelets ≥3501
Hb <10 g/dL or ESA use1
Pre-chemo Leukocytes >111
BMI ≥351
ScoreRiskProphylaxis
0LowNone
1-2IntermediateConsider (outpatient chemo)
≥3HighProphylactic LMWH/DOAC (AVERT, CASSINI trials)

Treatment of Established CAT

AgentEvidencePreferred In
LMWH (Dalteparin/Tinzaparin/Enoxaparin)CLOT Trial (Dalteparin vs Warfarin: ↓ recurrent VTE, no ↑ bleed)Historical gold standard
Rivaroxaban (15mg BD×21d→20mg OD)SELECT-D (Non-inferior for VTE, ↑ major bleed in GI/GU)Non-GI/GU cancers
Apixaban (10mg BD×7d→5mg BD)CARAVAGGIO (Non-inferior, less bleeding than LMWH)All cancers (preferred DOAC)
EdoxabanHokusai-Cancer (Non-inferior, ↑ bleeding)Alternative
[!tip] FCPS/MRCP: LMWH = Historical standard (CLOT). DOACs non-inferior: Apixaban preferred (CARAVAGGIO - less bleed); Avoid DOACs in GI/GU cancers (↑ bleed). Duration = Indefinite while cancer active.

Special Considerations in CAT

IssueManagement
Thrombocytopenia (Plt <50)Dose-reduce LMWH (50% if 25-50; hold if <25); DOACs avoid if <50
Renal ImpairmentLMWH dose-adjust; DOACs dose-adjust/avoid if CrCl <15-30
GI/GU CancersLMWH preferred (DOACs ↑ major bleed)
Brain TumoursLMWH (DOACs ↑ intracranial bleed risk)
Recurrent VTE on LMWHIncrease LMWH 20-25% or Switch to DOAC
Recurrent VTE on DOACSwitch to LMWH

💡 Massive PE & IVC Filter

Massive PE Definition & Treatment

DefinitionHaemodynamic instability: SBP <90 mmHg or drop >40 mmHg for >15 min, NOT due to other causes
ThrombolysisAlteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours (or 10mg bolus + 90mg over 90min)
IndicationsMassive PE (hemodynamic instability)
ContraindicationsActive bleed, recent surgery/stroke, intracranial pathology, aortic dissection
AlternativeSurgical embolectomy / Catheter-directed thrombolysis / Mechanical thrombectomy
Submassive PERV dysfunction + Troponin/BNP ↑ → Consider thrombolysis (PEITHO trial: ↓ haemodynamic collapse, ↑ bleed)

IVC Filter Indications

IndicationDetails
Contraindication to AnticoagulationActive bleed, recent neurosurgery, high bleed risk
Recurrent VTE on Therapeutic AnticoagulationConfirmed adherence + therapeutic levels
Massive PE with Contraindication to ThrombolysisBridge to anticoagulation
NOT forPrimary prophylaxis, routine CAT, transient risk factors
[!warning] IVC Filter = Temporary (prefer retrievable); Complications: Filter thrombosis, migration, perforation, IVC occlusion, DVT recurrence ↑

🔄 Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Cellulitis/ErysipelasUnilateral leg swelling + erythema + warmth + fever; US negative
Baker's Cyst RuptureCalf swelling, crescent sign on US; US shows cyst, no DVT
Superficial ThrombophlebitisPalpable cord, erythema along superficial vein; US confirms
Muscle Haematoma/TearTrauma history, localised pain; US shows haematoma
LymphoedemaChronic, non-pitting, no acute onset; Lymphoscintigraphy
Pneumonia/Heart Failure (Dyspnoea)CXR, BNP, Echo; CTPA if PE suspected

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DVT ProbabilityWells Score: Low/Moderate → D-dimer; High → US
PE ProbabilityWells/Geneva: Unlikely → D-dimer; Likely → CTPA
D-dimerAge-adjusted (Age × 10 if >50) for Low/Moderate prob
First-line VTE TreatmentDOACs (Rivaroxaban/Apixaban) – no LMWH overlap needed
Warfarin IndicationsMechanical valves, Pregnancy, AT Def, APS triple positive, CrCl <15-30
DurationProvoked 3mo; Unprovoked/CAT/APS = Indefinite
CAT TreatmentLMWH standard (CLOT); Apixaban preferred DOAC (CARAVAGGIO); Avoid DOACs in GI/GU (SELECT-D)
CAT DurationIndefinite while cancer active
Massive PEAlteplase 100mg IV 2h (haemodynamic instability)
IVC FilterAnticoagulation contraindicated OR Recurrent VTE on therapeutic AC

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the Wells DVT score and how does it guide imaging?
  • ≤0 Low → D-dimer (if neg exclude); 1-2 Moderate → D-dimer/US; ≥3 High → Direct US
  1. How does age-adjusted D-dimer improve VTE exclusion?
  • Cut-off = Age × 10 μg/L (if >50) – increases specificity without losing sensitivity
  1. What are the first-line treatments for acute VTE and their dosing?
  • Rivaroxaban 15mg BD×21d→20mg OD; Apixaban 10mg BD×7d→5mg BD; (No heparin overlap needed)
  1. When is warfarin preferred over DOACs for VTE?
  • Mechanical valves, Pregnancy, AT Deficiency, APS triple positive, Severe renal impairment (CrCl <15-30)
  1. What is the recommended duration for provoked vs unprovoked VTE?
  • Provoked: 3 months; Unprovoked: Indefinite (if bleed risk low)
  1. What is the standard treatment for Cancer-Associated Thrombosis (CAT)?
  • LMWH (CLOT trial); DOACs non-inferior: Apixaban preferred (CARAVAGGIO); Avoid DOACs in GI/GU cancers (↑ bleed)
  1. How long should anticoagulation continue in CAT?
  • Indefinite while cancer active
  1. What is the definition and treatment of Massive PE?
  • Haemodynamic instability (SBP<90); Alteplase 100mg IV over 2h
  1. When is an IVC filter indicated?
  • Contraindication to anticoagulation OR Recurrent VTE on therapeutic anticoagulation
  1. Differentiate the bleeding risk of DOACs vs LMWH in GI/GU cancers.
  • SELECT-D: Rivaroxaban ↑ major bleed in GI/GU; CARAVAGGIO: Apixaban similar bleed to LMWH (preferred if DOAC needed)

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Wells DVT vs PEDVT: Alternative diagnosis -2; PE: PE #1 diagnosis +3
D-dimer CutoffsStandard 500; Age-adjusted = Age×10 (if >50)
DOACs in CATApixaban preferred (CARAVAGGIO); Avoid in GI/GU (SELECT-D)
Duration: Provoked vs UnprovokedProvoked = 3mo; Unprovoked/CAT = Indefinite
Massive vs Submassive PEMassive = Haemodynamic instability → Thrombolysis; Submassive = RV strain → Consider
MnemonicMeaning
"Wells DVT: Alt Dx = -2"Wells DVT scoring
"D-dimer: Age×10 if >50"Age-adjusted cut-off
"DOACs = 1st Line VTE"First-line treatment
"CAT = LMWH (CLOT) / Apixaban (CARAVAGGIO)"Cancer thrombosis
"Indefinite = Unprovoked + CAT + APS"Duration rules
"Massive PE = SBP<90 = Alteplase 100mg"Massive PE treatment
"IVC Filter = No AC or Recurrent on AC"IVC filter indications

🗺️ Mind Map

VTE: DVT/PE & CAT
Diagnosis
DVT: Wells → D-dimer/US
PE: Wells/Geneva → D-dimer/CTPA
D-dimer: Age-adjusted (Age×10 >50)
Acute Treatment
DOACs: Riva/Apixa (1st line)
Warfarin: Mech valve, Preg, AT Def, APS triple
Duration
Provoked: 3mo
Unprovoked: Indefinite
CAT: Indefinite (cancer active)
APS: Indefinite
Cancer-Associated Thrombosis
Khorana Score (Prophylaxis)
LMWH: CLOT Trial (Standard)
DOACs: Apixaban CARAVAGGIO (Preferred)
Avoid DOAC in GI/GU (SELECT-D)
Massive PE
SBP<90 → Alteplase 100mg/2h
Submassive: RV strain → Consider
IVC Filter
Contraindication to AC
Recurrent VTE on Therapeutic AC

📋 One-Page Revision Card

VTE & CAT – FCPS/MRCP REVISION CARD
DVT Dx: Wells ≤0 Low → D-dimer; 1-2 Mod → D-dimer/US; ≥3 High → US
PE Dx: Wells/Geneva Unlikely → D-dimer; Likely → CTPA
D-dimer: Age-adjusted = Age×10 (if >50)
Rx: DOACs 1st line (Riva 15mg BD×21d→20mg; Apixa 10mg BD×7d→5mg)
Warfarin: Mech valve, Preg, AT Def, APS triple, CrCl<15
Duration: Provoked 3mo; Unprovoked/CAT/APS Indefinite
CAT: LMWH (CLOT); Apixaban (CARAVAGGIO); No DOAC in GI/GU
Massive PE: SBP<90 → Alteplase 100mg/2h
IVC Filter: Contraindication to AC / Recurrent VTE on AC
